items.title: Panic Disorder: Clinical Course, Differential Diagnosis, and Comorbidity
items.content:
Panic disorder is defined by recurrent, unpredictable panic attacks — sudden episodes of intense fear with palpitations, sweating, trembling, dyspnea, chest pain, dizziness, and fear of impending doom or death.
Attacks develop within 10 minutes and usually resolve within an hour; diagnosis requires at least 1 month of worry about further attacks or behavior change related to them.
Lifetime prevalence is 2-3%, with onset typically in late adolescence to early adulthood and the first attack usually occurring outside the home.
Agoraphobia — an acquired irrational fear of being trapped or unable to escape — commonly develops and progressively restricts the patient's lifestyle and geography.
Comorbidity is high: ~75% of panic disorder patients also meet criteria for major depression, and alcohol/benzodiazepine abuse often begins as self-medication.
Medical mimics (pheochromocytoma, thyrotoxicosis, hypoglycemia, paroxysmal atrial tachycardia, mitral valve prolapse) must be excluded; panic disorder was the primary diagnosis in 43% of patients with chest pain and normal coronary angiograms.
keyPoints: Attacks peak within 10 min, resolve in about an hour
Agoraphobia may be diagnosed even without panic disorder
~75% comorbidity with major depression
43% of chest-pain patients with normal angiograms have panic disorder as primary diagnosis
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Panic Disorder): "Panic attacks have a sudden onset, developing within 10 min and usually resolving over the course of an hour, and can occur in an unexpected fashion, such as when waking from sleep."

items.title: How sodium lactate, yohimbine, CCK-4, and CO2 provoke panic attacks via locus coeruleus-raphe circuits
items.content:
Panic disorder's pathophysiology is unknown but involves genetic predisposition, altered autonomic responsivity, and social learning.
The disorder shows familial aggregation, with 30-45% concordance in monozygotic twins, and genome-wide screens have identified suggestive risk loci.
Acute panic attacks are linked to increased noradrenergic discharge from the locus coeruleus.
Four pharmacologic/physiologic challenges reliably provoke panic attacks in susceptible patients: intravenous sodium lactate (in two-thirds of patients), the alpha2-adrenergic antagonist yohimbine, cholecystokinin tetrapeptide (CCK-4), and carbon dioxide inhalation.
Each is hypothesized to activate a pathway involving noradrenergic neurons in the locus coeruleus and serotonergic neurons in the dorsal raphe — explaining why both noradrenergic and serotonergic antidepressants are effective.
Resting-state fMRI shows default-mode-network abnormalities involving the medial temporal lobe, with greater sensorimotor cortex activation in panic disorder and altered amygdala-frontal connectivity in social anxiety disorder.
keyPoints: 30-45% monozygotic twin concordance
Sodium lactate provokes attacks in ~2/3 of patients
Yohimbine, CCK-4, and CO2 inhalation are also provocative challenges
Locus coeruleus (noradrenergic) + dorsal raphe (serotonergic) pathway implicated
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Panic Disorder): "Intravenous infusion of sodium lactate evokes an attack in two-thirds of panic disorder patients, as do the alpha2-adrenergic antagonist yohimbine, cholecystokinin tetrapeptide (CCK-4), and carbon dioxide inhalation."

items.title: Why agoraphobia develops as a learned consequence of recurrent panic attacks
items.content:
Agoraphobia is an acquired irrational fear of being in places where escape might be difficult or help unavailable if a panic attack recurs.
It develops because patients with anticipatory anxiety generalize their fear of an attack to the situations in which attacks have occurred, leading to progressive avoidance.
Over time this produces a literal restriction in lifestyle and geography — patients become housebound and dependent on companions to venture out.
Patients are frequently embarrassed about being housebound and do not volunteer this information, so physicians must ask directly or the syndrome will go unrecognized.
Importantly, agoraphobia is not exclusively a complication of panic disorder — it may be diagnosed even when panic disorder is absent, reflecting its status as a distinct anxiety phenotype.
keyPoints: Agoraphobia = fear of being trapped/unable to escape if a panic attack recurs
Develops via anticipatory anxiety and progressive situational avoidance
Can be diagnosed independent of panic disorder
Patients often hide housebound behavior — direct questioning required
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Panic Disorder): "Agoraphobia, which occurs commonly in patients with panic disorder, is an acquired irrational fear of being in places where one might feel trapped or unable to escape. It may, however, be diagnosed even if panic disorder is not present."

items.title: How SSRI starting doses for panic disorder differ from standard antidepressant dosing
items.content:
SSRIs benefit the majority of panic disorder patients without the cardiovascular and anticholinergic adverse effects of TCAs, and fluoxetine, paroxetine, sertraline, and the SNRI venlafaxine are FDA approved for panic disorder.
Because panic patients are exquisitely sensitive to somatic symptoms, starting at a full antidepressant dose can itself trigger anxiety/jitteriness that mimics a panic attack and causes early dropout.
The solution is to start at one-third to one-half of the usual antidepressant dose — e.g., fluoxetine 5-10 mg, sertraline 25-50 mg, paroxetine 10 mg, venlafaxine 37.5 mg — and titrate upward as tolerated.
Antidepressants take 2-6 weeks to become effective, so doses are adjusted gradually based on clinical response, often with a short-term benzodiazepine bridge for anticipatory anxiety.
keyPoints: SSRIs/SNRI (fluoxetine, paroxetine, sertraline, venlafaxine) are FDA approved for panic disorder
Start at 1/3 to 1/2 of usual antidepressant dose to avoid activation-induced anxiety
Example: fluoxetine 5-10 mg, sertraline 25-50 mg, paroxetine 10 mg, venlafaxine 37.5 mg
Full effect takes 2-6 weeks
items.reference: TREATMENT (Panic Disorder): "These drugs should be started at one-third to one-half of their usual antidepressant dose (e.g., 5-10 mg fluoxetine, 25-50 mg sertraline, 10 mg paroxetine, venlafaxine 37.5 mg)."

items.title: Why benzodiazepines should generally not be prescribed for more than 4-6 weeks
items.content:
Benzodiazepines (lorazepam, oxazepam, clonazepam, alprazolam) provide rapid relief in GAD and are useful early in treatment, started at the lowest effective dose and used as-needed.
However, prolonged use leads to pharmacologic tolerance — the anxiolytic effect wanes while dependence builds — and carries a serious risk of abuse, especially with rapidly absorbed, lipid-soluble agents like diazepam.
This is why guidelines limit benzodiazepine courses to roughly 4-6 weeks, after which withdrawal must be closely monitored because relapse of anxiety symptoms can occur during taper.
Longer-acting agents accumulate active metabolites causing sedation and cognitive/psychomotor impairment, while shorter-acting agents (alprazolam, lorazepam, oxazepam) can produce rebound anxiety and early-morning insomnia on discontinuation.
Withdrawal from longer half-life agents is managed by gradual stepwise dose reduction (10% every 1-2 weeks) over 6-12 weeks; shorter-acting agents may require switching to a longer half-life benzodiazepine or adding an adjunct (beta blocker, carbamazepine) before discontinuation.
keyPoints: Benzodiazepine course generally limited to 4-6 weeks
Risk: tolerance, dependence, abuse — especially with lipid-soluble, rapid-onset agents (diazepam)
Taper longer-acting agents by 10% every 1-2 weeks over 6-12 weeks
Withdrawal can cause depression, anxiety, autonomic arousal, rarely seizures
items.reference: TREATMENT (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "Benzodiazepines should generally not be prescribed for >4-6 weeks because of the development of tolerance and the serious risk of abuse and dependence."

items.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder: Diagnostic Features, Epidemiology, and Comorbidity
items.content:
GAD is characterized by persistent, excessive, unrealistic worry accompanied by muscle tension, impaired concentration, autonomic arousal, feeling 'on edge' or restless, and insomnia, present more days than not for at least 6 months.
Onset is usually before age 20, often with a history of childhood fears and social inhibition; lifetime prevalence is 5-6% and higher in first-degree relatives of affected individuals.
Family studies show GAD and panic disorder segregate independently — they are genetically distinct entities despite overlapping symptoms.
More than 80% of GAD patients also have major depression, dysthymia, or social phobia, and comorbid substance abuse (especially alcohol/sedative-hypnotics) is common.
Unlike panic disorder, GAD patients worry excessively over minor matters with life-disrupting effects, while complaints of shortness of breath, palpitations, and tachycardia are relatively rare.
keyPoints: Persistent excessive worry + 3 of 6 associated symptoms for ≥6 months (1 symptom suffices in children)
Lifetime prevalence 5-6%, onset usually before age 20
GAD and panic disorder segregate independently in families
>80% comorbidity with major depression, dysthymia, or social phobia
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "Patients with generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) have persistent, excessive, and/or unrealistic worry associated with muscle tension, impaired concentration, autonomic arousal, feeling 'on edge' or restless, and insomnia."

items.title: How GABA-A receptor subunit selectivity determines anxiolytic versus sedative effects of benzodiazepines
items.content:
Most anxiogenic and anxiolytic agents act on the GABA-A receptor/chloride ion channel complex, implicating GABA in the pathogenesis of anxiety.
Benzodiazepines bind two GABA-A receptor sites: type I (broad neuroanatomic distribution) and type II (concentrated in hippocampus, striatum, and neocortex).
The clinically desired antianxiety effects of various benzodiazepines correlate with their relative binding to the alpha-2 and alpha-3 subunits of the GABA-A receptor.
By contrast, sedation and memory impairment — the unwanted effects — correlate with binding to the alpha-1 subunit.
This explains why drug development for anxiety has sought subunit-selective compounds that retain anxiolytic efficacy (alpha-2/3) while minimizing sedation and amnesia (alpha-1); serotonin and 3alpha-reduced neuroactive steroids (allosteric GABA-A modulators) also contribute, which is why buspirone (5-HT1A partial agonist) and certain 5-HT2A/2C antagonists (mirtazapine, nefazodone) have anxiolytic benefit.
keyPoints: Benzodiazepines act at GABA-A receptor/chloride channel complex
Alpha-2/alpha-3 subunit binding = anxiolytic effect
Alpha-1 subunit binding = sedation and memory impairment
Serotonergic agents (buspirone, mirtazapine, nefazodone) also modulate anxiety via 5-HT receptors
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "The antianxiety effects of the various benzodiazepines are influenced by their relative binding to alpha 2 and 3 subunits of the GABAA receptor, and sedation and memory impairment to the alpha 1 subunit."

items.title: Why buspirone is a poor substitute for benzodiazepines in previously benzodiazepine-responsive patients
items.content:
Buspirone is a partial 5-HT1A receptor agonist used for GAD that has no abuse or disinhibition potential and does not bind benzodiazepine receptors or potentiate alcohol.
However, it requires several weeks to take effect and requires thrice-daily dosing — a markedly different onset profile from benzodiazepines, which work within minutes to hours.
Patients who have previously experienced the rapid, robust relief of a benzodiazepine tend to rate buspirone as less effective, even if its pharmacologic profile is appropriate for their condition.
Buspirone is, however, particularly useful in patients with head injury or dementia who have anxiety and/or agitation, where benzodiazepine-related sedation, disinhibition, or cognitive impairment would be especially problematic.
This illustrates a broader principle: patient expectations shaped by prior treatment response strongly influence perceived efficacy of a new agent, independent of its actual pharmacologic merit.
keyPoints: Buspirone = partial 5-HT1A agonist, no abuse potential, no benzodiazepine receptor binding
Onset takes weeks; requires thrice-daily dosing
Patients previously responsive to benzodiazepines rate it as less effective
Particularly useful in head injury/dementia patients with anxiety or agitation
items.reference: TREATMENT (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "Patients who were previously responsive to a benzodiazepine are unlikely to rate buspirone as equally effective, but patients with head injury or dementia who have symptoms of anxiety and/or agitation may do well with this agent."

items.subtopic: Phobic Disorders — Clinical Features and Treatment
items.title: Phobic Disorders: Clinical Features, Epidemiology, and Beta-Blocker Use
items.content:
Phobic disorders are characterized by marked, persistent fear of specific objects or situations that triggers an immediate anxiety reaction and leads to avoidance that impairs functioning.
Unlike other anxiety disorders, phobic patients usually experience anxiety only in specific situations; common phobias include claustrophobia, fear of blood, and fear of flying.
Social phobia is a specific fear of social/performance situations involving exposure to unfamiliar people or evaluation by others (e.g., conversing at a party, using public restrooms).
Phobic disorders affect ~7-9% of the population, with females affected twice as often as males; full criteria are usually met in early adulthood though behavioral avoidance often dates from childhood.
Beta blockers (e.g., propranolol 20-40 mg orally 2 h before the event) are particularly effective for 'performance anxiety' specifically (not general social phobia) by blocking peripheral manifestations — perspiration, tachycardia, palpitations, tremor.
MAOIs alleviate social phobia independently of antidepressant activity, and paroxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine CR, and venlafaxine XR are FDA approved for social anxiety.
keyPoints: 7-9% prevalence; females affected 2x as often as males
Social phobia = fear of social/performance situations involving scrutiny by others
Propranolol 20-40 mg PO 2h pre-event for performance anxiety specifically
Paroxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine CR, venlafaxine XR FDA approved for social anxiety
items.reference: TREATMENT (Phobic Disorders): "Beta blockers (e.g., propranolol, 20-40 mg orally 2 h before the event) are particularly effective in the treatment of 'performance anxiety' (but not general social phobia) and appear to work by blocking the peripheral manifestations of anxiety such as perspiration, tachycardia, palpitations, and tremor."

items.title: How fear conditioning and amygdala circuitry underlie the heritability of phobias
items.content:
Twin studies of female twins found concordance rates for agoraphobia, social phobia, and animal phobia of 23% in monozygotic versus 15% in dizygotic twins, indicating a genetic contribution to phobic vulnerability.
A twin study of fear conditioning — an animal model for the acquisition of phobias — demonstrated heritability of 35-45%, suggesting that the capacity to acquire conditioned fear responses is itself substantially genetically determined.
Animal studies of fear conditioning show that processing of the fear stimulus occurs through the lateral nucleus of the amygdala, extending through the central nucleus and projecting to the periaqueductal gray, lateral hypothalamus, and paraventricular hypothalamus.
This circuit explains the somatic/autonomic features of phobic reactions (the periaqueductal gray and hypothalamic projections drive freeze/flight responses and neuroendocrine activation), while the lateral-to-central amygdala pathway encodes the learned association between a cue and threat.
Together, the genetic and circuit-level findings suggest phobias arise from an inherited propensity for the amygdala fear-conditioning circuit to form unusually strong or persistent threat associations.
keyPoints: Monozygotic vs dizygotic concordance: 23% vs 15% for phobias
Fear conditioning heritability 35-45%
Amygdala circuit: lateral nucleus → central nucleus → periaqueductal gray, lateral/paraventricular hypothalamus
Circuit mediates both learned threat association and autonomic/neuroendocrine fear response
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Phobic Disorders): "Animal studies of fear conditioning have indicated that processing of the fear stimulus occurs through the lateral nucleus of the amygdala, extending through the central nucleus and projecting to the periaqueductal gray region, lateral hypothalamus, and paraventricular hypothalamus."

items.title: PTSD: Diagnostic Criteria Overview in Individuals Older Than 6 Years
items.content:
PTSD requires exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence — directly, by witnessing it happen to others, by learning it happened to a close family member/friend, or through repeated extreme occupational exposure to aversive details (e.g., first responders).
Intrusion symptoms (Criterion B) include recurrent distressing memories, nightmares, dissociative flashbacks, and intense psychological/physiological reactions to trauma cues.
Avoidance (Criterion C) of trauma-related thoughts, feelings, or external reminders must be present.
Negative alterations in cognition and mood (Criterion D) include dissociative amnesia for the event, persistent negative beliefs ('the world is completely dangerous'), self-blame, persistent negative emotions, diminished interest, detachment, and inability to experience positive emotions.
Marked alterations in arousal and reactivity (Criterion E) include irritability/anger outbursts, recklessness, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, concentration problems, and sleep disturbance.
Duration must exceed 1 month (Criterion F), cause significant distress/impairment (Criterion G), and not be attributable to a substance or other medical condition (Criterion H). A dissociative subtype (depersonalization/derealization) and a delayed-expression subtype (full criteria not met until ≥6 months post-event) can be specified.
keyPoints: Criterion A: exposure to death/injury/sexual violence (direct, witnessed, learned about, or occupational)
Criteria B-E: intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal
Duration >1 month (Criterion F)
Specifiers: with dissociative symptoms; with delayed expression (≥6 months)
items.reference: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Individuals Older Than 6 Years (Criterion A): "Exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence in one (or more) of the following ways: 1. Directly experiencing the traumatic event(s). 2. Witnessing, in person, the event(s) as it occurred to others..."

items.title: Why PTSD shows excessive noradrenergic activity but a blunted pituitary-adrenal response
items.content:
PTSD is hypothesized to involve excessive release of norepinephrine from the locus coeruleus in response to stress, with increased noradrenergic activity at projection sites in the hippocampus and amygdala.
This excess noradrenergic tone is thought to facilitate the encoding of fear-based memories — explaining the vivid, intrusive, and persistent nature of traumatic recollections in PTSD.
Despite this heightened sympathetic/noradrenergic responsiveness to trauma cues, the pituitary-adrenal (HPA axis) response is paradoxically blunted — a dissociation between the sympathetic and HPA stress systems that is distinctive to PTSD.
In addition to fear learning, PTSD involves changes in threat detection (insula overactivity), executive function, emotional regulation, and contextual learning.
Predictive biomarkers identified include increased heart rate and serum lactate, decreased coagulation, insulin resistance, and alterations in glycolysis and fatty acid uptake — reflecting a broader metabolic signature of chronic stress dysregulation.
keyPoints: Excess norepinephrine release from locus coeruleus → hippocampus/amygdala
Enhanced noradrenergic tone facilitates encoding of fear-based memories
Pituitary-adrenal (cortisol) response is blunted despite sympathetic hyperactivity
Insula overactivity affects threat detection; metabolic biomarkers (lactate, insulin resistance) are altered
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Stress Disorders): "It is hypothesized that in PTSD there is excessive release of norepinephrine from the locus coeruleus in response to stress and increased noradrenergic activity at projection sites in the hippocampus and amygdala... Greater sympathetic responses to cues associated with the traumatic event occur in PTSD, although pituitary adrenal responses are blunted."

items.title: How treatment of acute stress reactions differs from management of chronic PTSD
items.content:
Acute stress reactions occurring immediately after trauma are usually self-limited and are managed with short-term benzodiazepines plus supportive/expressive psychotherapy.
By contrast, PTSD — which is chronic and recurrent, and highly correlated with peritraumatic dissociative symptoms and acute stress disorder at the time of trauma — requires a more complex combined drug-and-behavioral approach.
Critically, attempts to prevent PTSD by giving escitalopram, hydrocortisone, or intranasal oxytocin during the acute stress period have proven equivocal, and benzodiazepines and SSRIs should NOT be given in the early aftermath of trauma despite their later role in chronic PTSD.
Once PTSD is established, SSRIs (paroxetine and sertraline are FDA approved), venlafaxine, fluoxetine, and topiramate reduce anxiety, intrusion, and avoidance; MDMA-assisted psychotherapy and stellate ganglion block have shown efficacy as adjuncts.
Low-dose trazodone and mirtazapine are used at night for insomnia, and trauma-focused cognitive-behavioral therapy, EMDR, and prolonged exposure therapy (including VR-augmented exposure) are the most effective psychotherapeutic strategies; debriefing after the traumatic event does not prevent PTSD and may worsen symptoms.
keyPoints: Acute stress disorder: short-term benzodiazepines + supportive psychotherapy
Benzodiazepines and SSRIs should NOT be given in the early post-trauma period
Established PTSD: SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline FDA approved), venlafaxine, fluoxetine, topiramate
Trauma-focused CBT, EMDR, and prolonged exposure are most effective; debriefing does not prevent PTSD
items.reference: TREATMENT (Stress Disorders): "Acute stress reactions are usually self-limited, and treatment typically involves the short-term use of benzodiazepines and supportive/expressive psychotherapy. The chronic and recurrent nature of PTSD, however, requires a more complex approach using drug and behavioral treatments... Benzodiazepines and SSRIs, however, should not be given in the early aftermath of trauma."

items.title: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: Clinical Features, Course, and Comorbidities
items.content:
OCD is characterized by obsessive thoughts and compulsive behaviors that impair everyday functioning; common themes include contamination/germ fears, handwashing, counting, and checking/rechecking (e.g., whether a door is locked).
By definition, obsessive-compulsive activities take up more than 1 hour per day and are performed to relieve the anxiety triggered by the core fear.
Patients often conceal symptoms out of embarrassment, so physicians must ask specific questions about recurrent thoughts/behaviors, especially if physical clues (chafed/reddened hands, patchy hair loss from trichotillomania) are present.
Comorbidities are common — most frequently depression, other anxiety disorders, eating disorders, and tics.
OCD has a lifetime prevalence of 2-3% worldwide, with gradual onset usually in early adulthood (though childhood onset is not rare) and a waxing-and-waning course; some cases show steady deterioration in psychosocial functioning.
keyPoints: Obsessive-compulsive activities consume >1 hour/day and relieve anxiety from a core fear
Common themes: contamination, handwashing, counting, checking
Lifetime prevalence 2-3%; onset usually early adulthood
Frequent comorbidities: depression, anxiety disorders, eating disorders, tics (and Tourette's in families)
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder): "in all cases, obsessive-compulsive activities take up >1 h per day and are undertaken to relieve the anxiety triggered by the core fear."

items.title: Why the orbitofrontal cortex-caudate-globus pallidus circuit is central to OCD pathophysiology
items.content:
A genetic contribution to OCD is suggested by twin studies, although no specific susceptibility gene has been identified; insulin signaling has been implicated in some recent reports.
Family studies show OCD aggregates with Tourette's disorder, and both are more common in males and in first-born children — suggesting shared developmental/genetic risk pathways.
The anatomy of obsessive-compulsive behavior centers on the orbitofrontal cortex, caudate nucleus, and globus pallidus — a cortico-striato-pallidal loop.
The caudate nucleus is specifically implicated in the acquisition and maintenance of habit and skill learning, which maps onto the repetitive, ritualized nature of compulsions as maladaptively 'overlearned' habits.
Critically, interventions that successfully reduce obsessive-compulsive behaviors (whether pharmacologic or behavioral) also decrease metabolic activity in the caudate — providing a biological marker of treatment response and reinforcing the caudate's causal role in symptom generation.
keyPoints: OCD aggregates with Tourette's disorder; more common in males and first-born children
Core circuit: orbitofrontal cortex - caudate nucleus - globus pallidus
Caudate mediates habit/skill learning, paralleling compulsive ritual behavior
Successful treatment reduces caudate metabolic activity
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder): "The anatomy of obsessive-compulsive behavior is thought to include the orbital frontal cortex, caudate nucleus, and globus pallidus. The caudate nucleus appears to be involved in the acquisition and maintenance of habit and skill learning, and interventions that are successful in reducing obsessive-compulsive behaviors also decrease metabolic activity in the caudate."

items.subtopic: Mood Disorders — Grief vs Major Depressive Episode
items.title: How to distinguish normal grief from a major depressive episode
items.content:
Grief and a major depressive episode (MDE) can share symptoms (sadness, insomnia, poor appetite, weight loss), but several features distinguish them.
In grief, the predominant affect is emptiness and loss; in an MDE, it is persistent depressed mood and an inability to anticipate happiness or pleasure.
Grief dysphoria tends to decrease in intensity over days to weeks and occurs in waves ('pangs of grief') tied to thoughts/reminders of the deceased, whereas MDE mood is more persistent and not tied to specific preoccupations.
Grief may be accompanied by positive emotions and humor, uncharacteristic of the pervasive unhappiness of an MDE.
Thought content in grief centers on memories of the deceased (and self-critical thoughts, if present, focus on perceived failings toward the deceased), whereas MDE features self-critical or pessimistic ruminations about oneself generally; self-esteem is generally preserved in grief but eroded in MDE.
If the bereaved has thoughts of death, in grief these focus on the deceased or 'joining' them, whereas in MDE such thoughts focus on ending one's own life due to feeling worthless or unable to cope — and clinical judgment is required because an MDE can coexist with, and be superimposed on, normal grief.
keyPoints: Grief = emptiness/loss in waves; MDE = persistent depressed mood, anhedonia
Self-esteem generally preserved in grief; eroded (worthlessness/self-loathing) in MDE
Grief allows positive emotions/humor; MDE does not
Death-related thoughts in grief focus on the deceased; in MDE focus on ending one's own life
items.reference: Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (discussion): "In distinguishing grief from a major depressive episode (MDE), it is useful to consider that in grief the predominant affect is feelings of emptiness and loss, while in an MDE it is persistent depressed mood and the inability to anticipate happiness or pleasure."

items.subtopic: Depression and Medical Illness — Cardiac Disease
items.title: Why depression in cardiac patients increases morbidity and mortality
items.content:
Between 20-30% of cardiac patients manifest a depressive disorder, with even higher rates when self-report scales are used.
Depressive symptoms following unstable angina, MI, cardiac bypass surgery, or heart transplant impair rehabilitation and are associated with higher mortality and medical morbidity.
Depressed patients show decreased heart rate variability — an index of reduced parasympathetic nervous system activity — which may predispose to ventricular arrhythmia and increased morbidity.
Depression also appears to independently increase the risk of coronary heart disease, possibly through increased platelet aggregation, creating a bidirectional relationship between depression and cardiac disease.
This mechanistic link is why drug choice matters: TCAs are contraindicated in bundle branch block and can cause tachycardia problematic in heart failure, whereas SSRIs do not induce ECG changes or adverse cardiac events (though they may potentiate anticoagulants via hepatic metabolism effects).
keyPoints: 20-30% of cardiac patients have a depressive disorder
Decreased HRV (reduced parasympathetic tone) → predisposition to ventricular arrhythmia
Depression increases coronary heart disease risk, possibly via increased platelet aggregation
Bidirectional relationship: depression worsens cardiac outcomes and cardiac disease worsens depression risk
items.reference: Depression in Association with Medical Illness: "Depressed patients often show decreased variability in heart rate (an index of reduced parasympathetic nervous system activity), which may predispose individuals to ventricular arrhythmia and increased morbidity. Depression also appears to increase the risk of coronary heart disease, possibly through increased platelet aggregation."

items.title: How neuroendocrine testing reveals abnormalities in major depression (cortisol, CRH, TRH-TSH)
items.content:
Major depression is associated with characteristic neuroendocrine abnormalities that reflect its neurovegetative signs and symptoms.
These include increased cortisol and corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) secretion — reflecting HPA-axis hyperactivity.
There is a decreased inhibitory response of glucocorticoids to dexamethasone — i.e., dexamethasone fails to normally suppress cortisol (the basis of the historic dexamethasone suppression test).
There is also a blunted TSH response to infusion of thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) — indicating altered hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis regulation.
Importantly, antidepressant treatment leads to normalization of these neuroendocrine abnormalities, suggesting they are markers of the depressed state rather than fixed trait abnormalities.
Major depression is also associated with changes in proinflammatory cytokines and neurotrophins, increased oxidative stress and cellular aging, telomere shortening, epigenetic changes, mitochondrial dysfunction, and alterations in the gut microbiome — and shows decreased REM latency, increased REM density, and decreased stage IV delta slow-wave sleep.
keyPoints: Increased cortisol and CRH secretion (HPA-axis hyperactivity)
Decreased glucocorticoid suppression by dexamethasone
Blunted TSH response to TRH infusion
Antidepressant treatment normalizes these neuroendocrine findings
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Depressive Disorders): "Neuroendocrine abnormalities that reflect the neurovegetative signs and symptoms of depression include increased cortisol and corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) secretion, a decreased inhibitory response of glucocorticoids to dexamethasone, and a blunted response of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level to infusion of thyroid-releasing hormone (TRH)."

items.subtopic: Depression Treatment — SSRIs vs TCAs in Cardiac Disease
items.title: Why SSRIs are preferred over TCAs for depression in patients with cardiac disease
items.content:
TCAs have antihistaminergic (sedating) and anticholinergic (constipation, dry mouth, urinary hesitancy, blurred vision) side effects, and they are contraindicated in patients with serious cardiovascular risk factors.
Specifically, TCAs are contraindicated in bundle branch block, and TCA-induced tachycardia is an additional concern in patients with congestive heart failure — both because TCAs have quinidine-like membrane effects that slow cardiac conduction and can precipitate arrhythmia or heart block.
SSRIs, by contrast, do not induce ECG changes or adverse cardiac events, making them reasonable first-line agents for patients at risk for TCA-related cardiac complications.
A caveat: SSRIs may interfere with hepatic metabolism of anticoagulants, causing increased anticoagulation — so cardiac patients on warfarin require monitoring when an SSRI is added.
This risk-benefit calculus (cardiac safety vs anticoagulant interaction) is a key part of individualizing antidepressant choice in patients with cardiovascular comorbidity.
keyPoints: TCAs contraindicated in bundle branch block; risk of tachycardia in CHF
SSRIs do not cause ECG changes or adverse cardiac events
SSRIs are reasonable first-line agents in patients at TCA-cardiac risk
Caveat: SSRIs may increase anticoagulant effect via hepatic metabolism interaction
items.reference: Depression in Association with Medical Illness: "TCAs are contraindicated in patients with bundle branch block, and TCA-induced tachycardia is an additional concern in patients with congestive heart failure. SSRIs appear not to induce ECG changes or adverse cardiac events and thus are reasonable first-line drugs for patients at risk for TCA-related complications."

items.title: How serotonin syndrome develops and presents clinically
items.content:
Serotonin syndrome is a risk of SSRIs (and other serotonergic agents) thought to result from hyperstimulation of brainstem 5-HT1A receptors.
It is characterized by myoclonus, agitation, abdominal cramping, hyperpyrexia, and hypertension, and can potentially be fatal.
The risk arises specifically when serotonergic agonists are taken in combination — for example, an SSRI combined with an MAOI, or with other serotonergic drugs — and such combinations should be monitored closely or avoided entirely.
This is the mechanistic basis for the rule that MAOIs should not be used concomitantly with SSRIs (risk of serotonin syndrome) or with TCAs (risk of hyperadrenergic effects).
Recognition is important because the presentation (agitation, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, neuromuscular abnormalities like myoclonus) can be mistaken for neuroleptic malignant syndrome or an infectious/toxidrome process, but the key distinguishing clue is recent initiation or combination of serotonergic medications.
keyPoints: Mechanism: hyperstimulation of brainstem 5-HT1A receptors
Features: myoclonus, agitation, abdominal cramping, hyperpyrexia, hypertension, potentially death
Risk highest when serotonergic agonists are combined (e.g., SSRI + MAOI)
MAOIs must not be combined with SSRIs (serotonin syndrome) or TCAs (hyperadrenergic effects)
items.reference: TREATMENT (Depressive Disorders): "One concern is the risk of 'serotonin syndrome,' which is thought to result from hyperstimulation of brainstem 5-HT1A receptors and is characterized by myoclonus, agitation, abdominal cramping, hyperpyrexia, hypertension, and potentially death."

items.title: Why TCA overdose is particularly dangerous and prescription quantity must be limited in suicidal patients
items.content:
TCAs are contraindicated in patients with serious cardiovascular risk factors because of their cardiac conduction effects, and overdoses of tricyclic agents can be lethal — with desipramine carrying the greatest risk.
Most patients require a daily dose of 150-200 mg of imipramine or amitriptyline (or equivalent) to achieve a therapeutic blood level of 150-300 ng/mL and satisfactory remission.
Because the therapeutic and toxic doses are not far apart, and because TCA overdose can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias and seizures, it is judicious to prescribe only a 10-day supply when suicide risk is present.
Newer antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs, etc.) are distinctly safer in overdose, which is a major reason they have become first-line despite TCAs not being completely superseded in efficacy.
This principle — matching drug toxicity profile to the patient's suicide risk — is a core element of rational antidepressant selection (Table 463-1) alongside side-effect profile and patient/family treatment history.
keyPoints: TCA overdose can be lethal; desipramine carries the greatest risk
Therapeutic TCA blood level 150-300 ng/mL achieved with ~150-200 mg/d imipramine/amitriptyline equivalent
Prescribe only a 10-day supply of TCAs when suicide is a risk
Newer antidepressants are safer in overdose — a key reason for first-line status
items.reference: TREATMENT (Depressive Disorders): "TCAs are contraindicated in patients with serious cardiovascular risk factors, and overdoses of tricyclic agents can be lethal, with desipramine carrying the greatest risk. It is judicious to prescribe only a 10-day supply when suicide is a risk."

items.title: How treatment-resistant depression is managed through augmentation and alternative strategies
items.content:
Regardless of treatment, response should be evaluated after ~2 months; about three-quarters of patients show improvement by then.
If remission is inadequate, the physician should assess compliance and consider increasing the dose if side effects allow, then refer to a mental health specialist if still unsuccessful.
Strategies for treatment resistance include: switching to an alternative antidepressant (a drug from the same or a different class is equally likely to be effective per the STAR-D trial), combining antidepressants, or adding adjunctive agents.
FDA-approved adjunctive options include certain atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine XR, aripiprazole, brexpiprazole) and the combination olanzapine/fluoxetine (Symbyax); other adjuncts include lithium, thyroid hormone, L-methylfolate, S-adenosylmethionine, N-acetyl cysteine, and dopamine agonists.
Patients whose SSRI response wanes may benefit from adding buspirone (10 mg tid), pindolol (2-5 mg tid), or a low-dose TCA such as nortriptyline (25 mg bid-tid).
Drug treatment should continue for at least 6-9 months to prevent relapse, and indefinite maintenance should be considered after two or more depressive episodes; non-pharmacologic options (ECT, rTMS, VNS, ketamine/esketamine, psilocybin, deep brain stimulation) are reserved for treatment-resistant or delusional cases.
keyPoints: Evaluate response at ~2 months; ~75% improve by then
STAR-D: switching within or across antidepressant classes shows similar efficacy
FDA-approved augmentation: quetiapine XR, aripiprazole, brexpiprazole, olanzapine/fluoxetine (Symbyax)
Continue treatment 6-9 months minimum; indefinite maintenance after ≥2 episodes
items.reference: TREATMENT (Depressive Disorders): "Strategies for treatment resistance include selection of an alternative drug, combinations of antidepressants, and/or adjunctive treatment with other classes of drugs, including lithium, thyroid hormone, L-methylfolate, S-adenosylmethionine, N-acetyl cysteine, atypical antipsychotic agents, and dopamine agonists."

items.title: Bipolar I Disorder: Manic Episode Diagnostic Criteria
items.content:
A manic episode requires a distinct period of abnormally and persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood plus abnormally and persistently increased activity/energy, lasting at least 1 week (or any duration if hospitalization is necessary), present most of the day, nearly every day.
During this period, three or more of the following must be present to a significant degree (four if mood is only irritable): inflated self-esteem/grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, increased talkativeness/pressure to keep talking, flight of ideas/racing thoughts, distractibility, increased goal-directed activity or psychomotor agitation, and excessive involvement in activities with high potential for painful consequences (buying sprees, sexual indiscretions, foolish business investments).
The disturbance must cause marked impairment in social/occupational functioning or necessitate hospitalization to prevent harm, or involve psychotic features.
The episode must not be attributable to a substance or another medical condition — except that a full manic episode emerging during antidepressant treatment that persists beyond the treatment's physiological effect IS sufficient for a manic episode diagnosis (and bipolar I diagnosis).
At least one lifetime manic episode is required for bipolar I; hypomanic episodes (similar criteria but ≥4 days, no marked impairment/hospitalization/psychosis) are common in bipolar I but not required for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Manic episode: ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalized) of elevated/expansive/irritable mood + increased energy
≥3 of 7 symptoms (≥4 if mood only irritable): grandiosity, ↓sleep need, pressured speech, flight of ideas, distractibility, goal-directed activity/agitation, risky behavior
A full manic episode emerging during antidepressant treatment that persists counts toward bipolar I diagnosis
Only 1 lifetime manic episode required for bipolar I; hypomania not required
items.reference: Manic Episode (Criterion A): "A distinct period of abnormally and persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood and abnormally and persistently increased activity or energy, lasting at least 1 week and present most of the day, nearly every day (or any duration if hospitalization is necessary)."

items.subtopic: Bipolar Disorder — Lithium and Suicide Risk
items.title: Why lithium uniquely reduces suicide risk among mood stabilizers
items.content:
Lithium carbonate is the mainstay of bipolar disorder treatment (though paradoxically underutilized), with a 70-80% response rate in acute mania and beneficial effects appearing within 1-2 weeks.
Lithium has a prophylactic effect against recurrent mania and, to a lesser extent, recurrent depression (which is generally harder to treat than unipolar depression).
A sustained blood lithium level of at least 0.8 meq/L is important for optimal prophylaxis AND has been shown to reduce the risk of suicide — a protective effect not yet demonstrated for other mood stabilizers (valproate, carbamazepine, atypical antipsychotics).
This unique antisuicidal property is a major reason lithium remains first-line despite its narrow therapeutic index (blood levels for therapeutic benefit, 0.8-1.2 meq/L, are close to toxic levels) and need for close monitoring.
Decreases in acute suicidal risk can also result from parenteral ketamine or intranasal esketamine, but lithium's effect on long-term suicide prevention during maintenance treatment is distinctive.
keyPoints: Lithium: 70-80% response in acute mania, effect within 1-2 weeks
Therapeutic level 0.8-1.2 meq/L; ≥0.8 meq/L needed for prophylaxis
Lithium uniquely reduces suicide risk — not yet shown for other mood stabilizers
Narrow therapeutic index requires close monitoring
items.reference: TREATMENT (Bipolar Disorder): "A sustained blood lithium level of at least 0.8 meq/L is important for optimal prophylaxis and has been shown to reduce the risk of suicide, a finding not yet apparent for other mood stabilizers."

items.title: How rapid-cycling bipolar disorder relates to thyroid dysfunction and antidepressant use
items.content:
Rapid cycling describes patients with four or more episodes of depression or mania within a given year, occurring in 15% of bipolar patients (most of whom are women).
In some cases, rapid cycling is linked to an underlying thyroid dysfunction — meaning thyroid function should be evaluated in any bipolar patient whose cycle frequency increases.
In other cases, rapid cycling is iatrogenically triggered by prolonged antidepressant treatment — antidepressants can accelerate cycling or precipitate mania, which is why their use is generally avoided during bipolar maintenance treatment.
Valproic acid may be more effective than lithium for patients with rapid cycling or mixed/dysphoric mania, making mood-stabilizer choice dependent on cycle pattern.
This dual etiology (endocrine vs iatrogenic) means evaluation of a rapid-cycling patient should include both thyroid function testing and a careful medication history, particularly antidepressant exposure.
keyPoints: Rapid cycling = ≥4 mood episodes/year; occurs in 15% of bipolar patients, mostly women
Can be linked to underlying thyroid dysfunction — check thyroid function
Can be iatrogenically triggered by prolonged antidepressant treatment
Valproate often preferred over lithium for rapid cycling or mixed/dysphoric mania
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Bipolar Disorder): "This pattern occurs in 15% of all patients, most of whom are women. In some cases, rapid cycling is linked to an underlying thyroid dysfunction, and in others, it is iatrogenically triggered by prolonged antidepressant treatment."

items.title: Somatic Symptom Disorder versus Factitious Disorder versus Malingering
items.content:
As many as 5-7% of patients in general medical practice experience a somatic symptom so distressing and preoccupying that it dominates their thoughts, feelings, and beliefs and impairs functioning — the diagnosis (somatic symptom disorder) rests on the patient's interpretation/elaboration of the symptom rather than the absence of medical explanation, and can coexist with well-established medical causation.
Illness anxiety disorder applies to individuals preoccupied with having/acquiring a serious illness without a specific somatic complaint; conversion disorder (functional neurologic symptom disorder) applies to unexplained symptoms of altered voluntary motor or sensory function.
Factitious disorder differs fundamentally: the patient consciously and voluntarily produces physical symptoms because the sick role itself is gratifying — common simulated presentations include chronic diarrhea, fever of unknown origin, GI bleeding/hematuria, seizures, and hypoglycemia, often not diagnosed until 5-10 years after onset.
Munchausen's syndrome is reserved for particularly dramatic, chronic, severe factitious illness.
Malingering is distinct from both: the fabrication of symptoms derives from a desire for an external reward, such as narcotic medication or disability reimbursement — i.e., a clear secondary gain motive rather than an intrinsic need for the sick role.
keyPoints: Somatic symptom disorder: 5-7% prevalence; defined by distress/preoccupation, can coexist with real medical disease
Illness anxiety disorder: preoccupation with serious illness without a specific complaint
Factitious disorder: consciously produced symptoms because the sick role is gratifying (Munchausen's = severe/chronic form)
Malingering: symptom fabrication for external reward (e.g., narcotics, disability payments)
items.reference: Somatic Symptom Disorder: "In factitious illnesses, the patient consciously and voluntarily produces physical symptoms of illness. The term Munchausen's syndrome is reserved for individuals with particularly dramatic, chronic, or severe factitious illness... In malingering, the fabrication derives from a desire for some external reward such as a narcotic medication or disability reimbursement."

items.title: Why anorexia nervosa carries the highest mortality among psychiatric disorders
items.content:
Anorexia nervosa is diagnosed when caloric restriction causes body weight to deviate significantly from age/gender/health/developmental norms, accompanied by fear of gaining weight and disturbed body image; restricting and binge-eating/purging subtypes exist and individuals may transition between them.
Patients frequently lack insight, are not comforted by achieved weight loss, and persist in behaviors despite meeting self-designated weight goals — reflecting altered reward-sensitivity and executive-function circuitry (frontal cortex, anterior insula) governing interoceptive awareness of hunger/satiety.
Medical consequences are multisystemic and life-threatening: leukopenia with lymphocytosis, elevated BUN, metabolic alkalosis and hypokalemia (with purging), amenorrhea, lanugo hair, hypotension, hypothermia, sinus bradycardia, hypogonadism, growth hormone resistance, and hypercortisolemia; osteoporosis is a longer-term concern.
Untreated anorexia has a mortality of 5.1/1000 — the highest among psychiatric conditions — driven by this combination of severe metabolic, cardiovascular, and endocrine derangement plus high suicide risk.
Treatment uses MANTRA and eating disorder-focused CBT with strict behavioral contingencies when weight loss is critical; no pharmacologic intervention is specifically beneficial for anorexia itself (though comorbid depression/anxiety should be treated), and weight gain must be gradual (0.5-1 lb/week) to prevent refeeding syndrome. Most achieve remission within 5 years.
keyPoints: Untreated mortality 5.1/1000 — highest of any psychiatric disorder
Medical complications: leukopenia, metabolic alkalosis/hypokalemia (purging), amenorrhea, bradycardia, hypercortisolemia
No specific pharmacotherapy; MANTRA and eating-disorder-focused CBT are effective
Weight gain goal 0.5-1 lb/week to avoid refeeding syndrome
items.reference: Anorexia Nervosa: "Untreated anorexia has a mortality of 5.1/1000, the highest among psychiatric conditions."

items.title: How clozapine differs from other antipsychotics in efficacy and monitoring requirements
items.content:
Clozapine is the model 'atypical' antipsychotic — a dibenzodiazepine with greater potency at blocking 5-HT2 than D2 receptors and a much higher affinity for D4 than D3 receptors.
Approximately 30% of patients who do not respond to conventional antipsychotics will respond to clozapine, and it has demonstrated superiority over other antipsychotics in preventing suicide — but its side-effect profile restricts it to treatment-resistant cases.
Unlike other antipsychotics, clozapine does not cause a rise in prolactin levels — distinguishing it from agents with stronger D2 blockade.
Its most serious adverse effects are agranulocytosis (incidence 1%) and seizure induction (incidence 10%), which is why weekly white blood cell counts are required, particularly during the first 3 months of treatment.
In treatment-resistant schizophrenia, transitioning to clozapine usually produces rapid improvement, though a prolonged delay in response in some cases necessitates a 6-9 month trial for maximal benefit; clozapine, olanzapine, and quetiapine are also the agents most likely to cause hyperglycemia, weight gain, and hypertriglyceridemia among second-generation antipsychotics.
keyPoints: Clozapine: ~30% response in treatment-resistant patients; superior suicide prevention
Does not raise prolactin (unlike other antipsychotics)
Agranulocytosis (1%) and seizures (10%) require weekly WBC monitoring, especially first 3 months
Reserved for treatment-resistant cases; may need 6-9 month trial for full benefit
items.reference: TREATMENT (Schizophrenia): "The most serious adverse effects of clozapine are agranulocytosis, which has an incidence of 1%, and induction of seizures, which has an incidence of 10%. Weekly white blood cell counts are required, particularly during the first 3 months of treatment."

items.question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with recurrent episodes of chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, and a sense of impending doom that develop abruptly and resolve within an hour. Cardiac workup including ECG and coronary angiography is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Discrete, abrupt-onset episodes of intense fear with palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, and a sense of doom that resolve within about an hour are classic for panic attacks. Studies show panic disorder is the primary diagnosis in a large proportion of patients with chest pain and normal coronary angiograms. GAD presents with chronic worry rather than discrete attacks; somatic symptom disorder involves preoccupation with somatic complaints rather than discrete panic episodes; hyperthyroidism should be excluded but the episodic, self-resolving pattern with normal workup favors panic disorder.
items.reference: Differential Diagnosis (Panic Disorder): "In two studies, panic disorder was the primary diagnosis in 43% of patients with chest pain who had normal coronary angiograms and was present in 9% of all outpatients referred for cardiac evaluation."

items.question: A patient with panic disorder is started on fluoxetine for the first time. To minimize the risk of an early activation/jitteriness reaction that could mimic a panic attack, which initial dosing strategy is most appropriate?
options: Start at the standard antidepressant dose of 20 mg/day
Start at one-third to one-half of the usual antidepressant dose (e.g., 5-10 mg/day) and titrate up
Start with a monoamine oxidase inhibitor instead
Start at twice the usual antidepressant dose for rapid effect
items.explanation: Panic disorder patients are exquisitely sensitive to somatic symptoms, so starting SSRIs at the usual antidepressant dose can trigger an activation reaction resembling a panic attack and lead to early discontinuation. The recommended approach is to begin at one-third to one-half of the usual antidepressant dose (e.g., fluoxetine 5-10 mg) and titrate based on response.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Panic Disorder): "These drugs should be started at one-third to one-half of their usual antidepressant dose (e.g., 5-10 mg fluoxetine, 25-50 mg sertraline, 10 mg paroxetine, venlafaxine 37.5 mg)."

items.question: A 28-year-old woman reports nearly daily, excessive worry about work, finances, and her family's health for the past year, accompanied by muscle tension, restlessness, and insomnia. She denies discrete episodes of palpitations or shortness of breath. Which feature most strongly distinguishes her presentation from panic disorder?
options: Chronic, diffuse worry without discrete attacks of palpitations/tachycardia
items.explanation: GAD is characterized by persistent, excessive worry over minor matters with associated muscle tension, impaired concentration, and insomnia, while complaints of shortness of breath, palpitations, and tachycardia (hallmarks of panic attacks) are relatively rare. Comorbid depression and family history occur in both conditions and do not distinguish them; both can have early-adulthood onset.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "Patients with GAD worry excessively over minor matters, with life-disrupting effects; unlike panic disorder, complaints of shortness of breath, palpitations, and tachycardia are relatively rare."

items.subtopic: GAD — Benzodiazepine Selection in Hepatic Impairment
items.question: A 70-year-old man with cirrhosis and impaired hepatic synthetic function requires short-term benzodiazepine therapy for severe anxiety. Which agents are preferred because they are metabolized via conjugation rather than oxidation and thus do not accumulate with impaired hepatic function?
items.explanation: Lorazepam and oxazepam are metabolized via conjugation (glucuronidation) rather than oxidative (CYP450) pathways, so they do not accumulate in patients with impaired hepatic function. Diazepam, chlordiazepoxide, flurazepam, and clonazepam undergo oxidative metabolism and accumulate active metabolites, risking toxicity in hepatic impairment.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "(The first two of these agents are metabolized via conjugation rather than oxidation and thus do not accumulate if hepatic function is impaired; the latter also has limited active metabolites.)"

items.question: A concert violinist experiences severe tremor, palpitations, and sweating only before public performances, with no other social anxiety symptoms. Which medication, taken shortly before the performance, is most appropriate?
options: Propranolol 20-40 mg orally 2 hours before the event
items.explanation: Beta blockers such as propranolol (20-40 mg orally 2 hours before the event) are particularly effective for 'performance anxiety' specifically — but not generalized social phobia — by blocking the peripheral manifestations of anxiety (tremor, tachycardia, sweating, palpitations). Fluoxetine is used for chronic social anxiety, not situational performance anxiety; clozapine and lithium have no role here.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Phobic Disorders): "Beta blockers (e.g., propranolol, 20-40 mg orally 2 h before the event) are particularly effective in the treatment of 'performance anxiety' (but not general social phobia) and appear to work by blocking the peripheral manifestations of anxiety such as perspiration, tachycardia, palpitations, and tremor."

items.question: A 25-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 6 hours after a serious motor vehicle accident. He is acutely anxious and distressed but has no prior psychiatric history. Which statement about pharmacologic management in this immediate post-trauma period is correct?
options: SSRIs should be started immediately to prevent PTSD
Benzodiazepines should be started immediately and continued long-term to prevent PTSD
Neither benzodiazepines nor SSRIs should be given in the early aftermath of trauma, as attempts at prevention with such agents have proven equivocal
MDMA-assisted psychotherapy should be initiated within the first 24 hours
items.explanation: Benzodiazepines and SSRIs should not be given in the early aftermath of trauma; attempts to prevent or ameliorate PTSD with agents such as escitalopram, hydrocortisone, or intranasal oxytocin during the acute stress period have proven equivocal. Acute stress reactions are managed with short-term supportive/expressive psychotherapy (benzodiazepines may be used very short-term for acute stress reactions, but not as PTSD-prevention strategy). MDMA-assisted psychotherapy is an adjunct used later in established PTSD treatment, not in the first 24 hours.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Stress Disorders): "Attempts to prevent or ameliorate PTSD through usage of agents such as escitalopram, hydrocortisone, and intranasal oxytocin in the acute stress period have proven equivocal... Benzodiazepines and SSRIs, however, should not be given in the early aftermath of trauma."

items.question: A patient with severe OCD is started on clomipramine. Which statement best describes its therapeutic mechanism in this condition?
options: Its efficacy in OCD is entirely dependent on its antidepressant activity
Its efficacy in OCD is unrelated to its antidepressant activity
It works only by blocking dopamine D2 receptors
It is ineffective in OCD and used only for comorbid depression
items.explanation: Clomipramine, a TCA, is approved for OCD, but its efficacy in OCD is unrelated to its antidepressant activity — it works through a distinct serotonergic mechanism relevant to the orbitofrontal-caudate-globus pallidus circuit implicated in OCD, independent of mood effects. It is often poorly tolerated due to anticholinergic/sedative effects at the doses required (25-250 mg/d).
items.reference: TREATMENT (Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder): "Clomipramine is a TCA that is often tolerated poorly owing to anticholinergic and sedative side effects at the doses required to treat the illness (25-250 mg/d); its efficacy in OCD is unrelated to its antidepressant activity."

items.question: A 60-year-old woman, two months after the death of her husband, reports persistent sadness, poor sleep, and reduced appetite. She describes the sadness as coming in waves triggered by memories of her husband, retains interest in seeing her grandchildren, and occasionally laughs while reminiscing. Which of the following best supports a diagnosis of normal grief rather than a major depressive episode?
options: Pervasive feelings of worthlessness and self-loathing
Persistent depressed mood unrelated to specific thoughts, with inability to anticipate any pleasure
Dysphoria occurring in waves tied to reminders of the deceased, with preserved capacity for positive emotions
Recurrent thoughts of ending her own life due to feeling undeserving of life
items.explanation: In grief, dysphoria tends to occur in waves ('pangs of grief') associated with thoughts or reminders of the deceased, and positive emotions/humor may still occur — both features described in this vignette. Pervasive worthlessness, persistent unrelenting depressed mood with inability to anticipate pleasure, and self-directed suicidal ideation due to feeling worthless are characteristic of a major depressive episode, not normal grief.
items.reference: Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (discussion): "The dysphoria in grief is likely to decrease in intensity over days to weeks and occurs in waves, the so-called pangs of grief... The pain of grief may be accompanied by positive emotions and humor that are uncharacteristic of the pervasive unhappiness and misery characteristic of an MDE."

items.question: Compared to patients with advanced gastric cancer, patients with pancreatic cancer show which of the following with respect to depression?
options: A lower prevalence of depression, attributable to less cachexia
A similar prevalence of depression, entirely explained by cachexia
A higher prevalence of depression that persists even when compared to patients with advanced gastric cancer, indicating cachexia alone does not explain the association
No meaningful difference, as depression prevalence in cancer patients is uniformly 25% regardless of cancer type
items.explanation: While the mean prevalence of depression in cancer patients is 25%, depression occurs in 40-50% of patients with cancers of the pancreas or oropharynx. This elevated association in pancreatic cancer is not due to cachexia alone, since the higher prevalence persists compared to patients with advanced gastric cancer (who also experience cachexia).
items.reference: Depression in Association with Medical Illness: "In patients with cancer, the mean prevalence of depression is 25%, but depression occurs in 40-50% of patients with cancers of the pancreas or oropharynx. This association is not due to the effect of cachexia alone, as the higher prevalence of depression in patients with pancreatic cancer persists when compared to those with advanced gastric cancer."

items.question: A patient with type 2 diabetes and major depression is being started on an antidepressant. Which class of agents may reduce fasting plasma glucose and improve dietary/medication compliance, while being easier to use than an alternative class with similar glycemic effects?
options: Tricyclic antidepressants, which can produce hyperglycemia and carbohydrate craving
SSRIs and SNRIs, which like MAOIs may reduce fasting plasma glucose but are easier to use
MAOIs, which are first-line due to favorable glycemic effects
Benzodiazepines, which have no effect on glycemic control
items.explanation: SSRIs and SNRIs, like MAOIs, may reduce fasting plasma glucose and improve compliance, but are easier to use than MAOIs (which carry dietary restrictions and hypertensive crisis risk). TCAs can produce hyperglycemia and carbohydrate craving, making them less favorable in diabetic patients.
items.reference: Depression in Association with Medical Illness: "MAOIs can induce hypoglycemia and weight gain, whereas TCAs can produce hyperglycemia and carbohydrate craving. SSRIs and SNRIs, like MAOIs, may reduce fasting plasma glucose but are easier to use and may also improve dietary and medication compliance."

items.question: A 68-year-old woman presents with depressed mood, memory impairment, fatigue, and weight gain over several months. Laboratory evaluation reveals an elevated TSH. What is the most appropriate initial management of her mood symptoms?
options: Start a TCA at full antidepressant dose immediately
Start thyroid hormone replacement, as improvement in mood usually follows normalization of thyroid function
Reassure the patient that thyroid status is unrelated to mood and proceed directly to antidepressant monotherapy
items.explanation: Hypothyroidism is frequently associated with depressed mood and memory impairment, and improvement in mood usually follows normalization of thyroid function with thyroid hormone replacement; adjunctive antidepressant medication is sometimes required but thyroid correction is the first step. Subclinical hypothyroidism can also produce depressive/cognitive symptoms responsive to thyroid replacement.
items.reference: Depression in Association with Medical Illness: "Hypothyroidism is frequently associated with features of depression, most commonly depressed mood and memory impairment... Improvement in mood usually follows normalization of thyroid function, but adjunctive antidepressant medication is sometimes required."

items.question: A patient on a stable SSRI dose is started on an MAOI for treatment-resistant depression without an adequate washout period. Within days, he develops myoclonus, agitation, abdominal cramping, hyperpyrexia, and hypertension. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Serotonin syndrome results from hyperstimulation of brainstem 5-HT1A receptors and presents with myoclonus, agitation, abdominal cramping, hyperpyrexia, and hypertension, potentially progressing to death. It is a key reason MAOIs should not be used concomitantly with SSRIs. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome involves antipsychotic exposure with muscular rigidity and elevated CPK; the combination of an SSRI with an MAOI without washout is the classic precipitant of serotonin syndrome.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Depressive Disorders): "One concern is the risk of 'serotonin syndrome,' which is thought to result from hyperstimulation of brainstem 5-HT1A receptors and is characterized by myoclonus, agitation, abdominal cramping, hyperpyrexia, hypertension, and potentially death... MAOIs should not be used concomitantly with SSRIs."

items.question: A patient with major depression and active suicidal ideation is being started on a TCA as part of a combined treatment plan. Which prescribing practice is most appropriate to mitigate overdose risk?
options: Provide a 3-month supply to ensure continuity of care
Choose desipramine preferentially, as it has the lowest overdose risk among TCAs
Prescribe only a 10-day supply at a time
Prescribe the full therapeutic dose (150-200 mg/day equivalent) for a 90-day supply upfront
items.explanation: Because TCA overdose can be lethal (with desipramine carrying the greatest risk, not the lowest), it is judicious to prescribe only a 10-day supply at a time when suicide is a risk, allowing closer monitoring while still providing therapeutic medication.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Depressive Disorders): "TCAs are contraindicated in patients with serious cardiovascular risk factors, and overdoses of tricyclic agents can be lethal, with desipramine carrying the greatest risk. It is judicious to prescribe only a 10-day supply when suicide is a risk."

items.question: A patient with major depression has had a partial response to an SSRI after 8 weeks at an adequate dose. Which of the following augmentation strategies has received FDA approval for treatment-resistant depression?
options: Adding a benzodiazepine as monotherapy replacement
Switching to an MAOI without a washout period
Doubling the SSRI dose beyond the maximum labeled dose
items.explanation: Adjunctive use of certain atypical antipsychotics — quetiapine extended-release, aripiprazole, and brexpiprazole — has received FDA approval for augmentation in treatment-resistant depression, as has the combination olanzapine/fluoxetine (Symbyax). Switching to an MAOI without washout risks serotonin syndrome; benzodiazepines are not an antidepressant augmentation strategy; exceeding labeled maximum doses is not a recommended approach.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Depressive Disorders): "the addition of certain atypical antipsychotic drugs (quetiapine extended-release; aripiprazole; brexpiprazole) has received FDA approval, as has usage of a combined medication, olanzapine and fluoxetine (Symbyax)."

items.question: A patient with bipolar I disorder achieves stable mood on lithium with a serum level of 0.5 meq/L but continues to have occasional depressive symptoms. Which adjustment is most consistent with optimizing both mood prophylaxis and suicide risk reduction?
options: Discontinue lithium since the patient is already stable
Increase the lithium dose to sustain a level of at least 0.8 meq/L
Add an SSRI without continuing lithium
items.explanation: A sustained blood lithium level of at least 0.8 meq/L is important for optimal prophylaxis against recurrent mania and depression and has been shown to reduce suicide risk, an effect not yet demonstrated for other mood stabilizers. Discontinuing lithium or switching to an antipsychotic without addressing the subtherapeutic level would not optimize prophylaxis.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Bipolar Disorder): "A sustained blood lithium level of at least 0.8 meq/L is important for optimal prophylaxis and has been shown to reduce the risk of suicide, a finding not yet apparent for other mood stabilizers."

items.question: A 38-year-old woman with bipolar disorder reports that her mood episodes have increased from one per year to five per year over the past 12 months. Which evaluation is most important at this point?
items.explanation: Rapid cycling (≥4 episodes/year) occurs in 15% of bipolar patients, mostly women, and in some cases is linked to underlying thyroid dysfunction. Thyroid function should be evaluated. A careful medication history (especially antidepressant exposure, which can iatrogenically trigger rapid cycling) is also warranted, but among the choices given, thyroid testing is the key initial evaluation.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Bipolar Disorder): "This pattern occurs in 15% of all patients, most of whom are women. In some cases, rapid cycling is linked to an underlying thyroid dysfunction, and in others, it is iatrogenically triggered by prolonged antidepressant treatment."

items.question: A patient being treated for major depressive disorder with an SSRI develops a full manic episode that persists well beyond the expected pharmacologic effect of the medication. How should this episode be classified diagnostically?
options: It cannot count toward a bipolar diagnosis because it was substance/medication-induced
It is sufficient evidence for a manic episode and bipolar I disorder diagnosis
It should be diagnosed as a substance-induced mood disorder only, regardless of duration
It indicates the patient has schizoaffective disorder
items.explanation: Per DSM-5 criteria, a full manic episode that emerges during antidepressant treatment but persists at a fully syndromal level beyond the physiological effect of that treatment is sufficient evidence for a manic episode (and therefore a bipolar I diagnosis) — it is not simply attributed to the medication.
items.reference: Manic Episode (Criterion B, note): "Note: A full manic episode that emerges during antidepressant treatment (e.g., medication, electroconvulsive therapy) but persists at a fully syndromal level beyond the physiological effect of that treatment is sufficient evidence for a manic episode... diagnosis."

items.question: A severely malnourished patient with anorexia nervosa is being admitted for nutritional rehabilitation. To minimize the risk of refeeding syndrome, which approach to weight restoration is most appropriate?
options: Rapid weight gain of 3-5 lb per week
Gradual weight gain of approximately 0.5-1 lb per week
Immediate resumption of full caloric intake on day one
Weight restoration is not a treatment priority in anorexia nervosa
items.explanation: Weight gain must occur gradually (approximately 0.5-1 lb/week) to prevent refeeding syndrome, a potentially fatal complication of overly rapid nutritional repletion in severely malnourished patients. Rapid refeeding risks severe electrolyte shifts (hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) and cardiac complications.
items.reference: Anorexia Nervosa (Treatment): "weight gain must be gradual (0.5-1 lb/week) to prevent refeeding syndrome."

items.subtopic: Eating Disorders — Bulimia Nervosa Medical Complications
items.question: A 22-year-old woman with bulimia nervosa who induces vomiting after binge-eating episodes presents with muscle weakness and an arrhythmia. Which electrolyte/acid-base abnormality is most likely responsible?
items.explanation: Purging behaviors (self-induced vomiting) cause metabolic alkalosis and hypokalemia due to loss of gastric hydrochloric acid and potassium, which can produce muscle weakness and cardiac arrhythmias. This is a key medical complication to recognize in eating disorders with purging subtypes.
items.reference: Anorexia Nervosa (Medical Complications): "metabolic alkalosis and hypokalemia (with purging)"

items.question: Which of the following best characterizes the mortality of untreated anorexia nervosa relative to other psychiatric disorders?
options: It has the lowest mortality of any psychiatric disorder
It has the highest mortality among psychiatric conditions, at 5.1 per 1000
Mortality is exclusively due to cardiac arrhythmia
items.explanation: Untreated anorexia nervosa has a mortality of 5.1 per 1000, the highest among psychiatric conditions, reflecting the combined effects of severe metabolic, cardiovascular, and endocrine derangement along with elevated suicide risk.
items.reference: Anorexia Nervosa: "Untreated anorexia has a mortality of 5.1/1000, the highest among psychiatric conditions."

items.question: A young woman has a long-standing pattern of unstable relationships, impulsivity, recurrent self-harm, chronic feelings of emptiness, and intense fear of abandonment. Which personality disorder cluster does this pattern represent?
options: Not a personality disorder, as symptoms must be episodic
items.explanation: Borderline personality disorder, characterized by instability in relationships, self-image, and affect along with marked impulsivity, fear of abandonment, and recurrent self-harm, belongs to Cluster B (dramatic, emotional, erratic), which also includes antisocial, histrionic, and narcissistic personality disorders. By definition, personality disorders represent enduring, pervasive patterns, not episodic states.
items.reference: Personality Disorders: "Personality disorders have been grouped into three overlapping clusters... Cluster B disorders include antisocial, borderline, histrionic, and narcissistic types and describe individuals whose behavior is impulsive, excessively emotional, and erratic."

items.question: A patient with treatment-resistant schizophrenia is started on clozapine. Which monitoring protocol is required, particularly during the first 3 months of treatment, because of its most serious adverse effect?
options: Weekly white blood cell counts to monitor for agranulocytosis
Daily liver function tests for hepatotoxicity
Monthly prolactin levels, since clozapine markedly raises prolactin
Continuous ECG monitoring for QT prolongation as the primary concern
items.explanation: Clozapine's most serious adverse effects are agranulocytosis (incidence 1%) and seizure induction (incidence 10%); weekly white blood cell counts are required, particularly during the first 3 months of treatment. Unlike other antipsychotics, clozapine does not raise prolactin levels.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Schizophrenia): "The most serious adverse effects of clozapine are agranulocytosis, which has an incidence of 1%, and induction of seizures, which has an incidence of 10%. Weekly white blood cell counts are required, particularly during the first 3 months of treatment."

items.question: Approximately what proportion of patients who fail to respond to conventional (first-generation) antipsychotics will respond to clozapine?
items.explanation: Approximately 30% of patients who do not respond to conventional antipsychotics will respond to clozapine, which has also demonstrated superiority over other antipsychotics in preventing suicide — properties that justify its use despite its more demanding side-effect monitoring.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Schizophrenia): "Approximately 30% of patients who do not respond to conventional antipsychotics will respond to clozapine, and clozapine has also demonstrated superiority over other antipsychotics in the prevention of suicide."

items.question: A patient with schizophrenia on long-term antipsychotic therapy develops significant weight gain, hyperglycemia, and hypertriglyceridemia. Which agents among second-generation antipsychotics are most strongly associated with these metabolic effects?
items.explanation: Among second-generation antipsychotics, clozapine, olanzapine, and quetiapine are most likely to cause hyperglycemia, weight gain, and hypertriglyceridemia, requiring metabolic monitoring (weight, glucose, lipids) during long-term therapy.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Schizophrenia): "clozapine, olanzapine, and quetiapine are also the agents most likely to cause hyperglycemia, weight gain, and hypertriglyceridemia among second-generation antipsychotics."

items.subtopic: Assessment of Violence — Victims of Abuse
items.question: A primary care physician suspects a patient is a victim of ongoing spousal abuse. Compared to the general population, this patient is more likely to also be affected by which of the following?
options: Hypertension only, with no psychiatric comorbidity
Depression, anxiety, substance abuse, and a history of suicide attempts
No increased psychiatric risk, as abuse has no documented psychological correlates
items.explanation: An interview study of 24,000 women in 10 countries found a lifetime prevalence of physical or sexual violence ranging from 15-71%, and these individuals are more likely to suffer from depression, anxiety, and substance abuse and to have attempted suicide. Abused individuals also frequently express low self-esteem, vague somatic symptoms, social isolation, and a passive feeling of loss of control.
items.reference: ASSESSMENT AND EVALUATION OF VIOLENCE: "An interview study of 24,000 women in 10 countries found a lifetime prevalence of physical or sexual violence that ranged from 15-71%; these individuals are more likely to suffer from depression, anxiety, and substance abuse and to have attempted suicide."

items.subtopic: Somatic Symptom Disorder vs Factitious Disorder vs Malingering
