items.question: A patient repeatedly presents with factitious episodes of hypoglycemia caused by surreptitious insulin injection, motivated by a desire to remain in the hospital and receive medical attention. Which diagnosis best fits?
items.explanation: In factitious disorder, the patient consciously and voluntarily produces physical symptoms of illness because the sick role itself is gratifying — common presentations include factitious hypoglycemia, GI bleeding, fever of unknown origin, and seizures. Malingering instead involves fabrication for an external reward (e.g., disability payments, narcotics), which is not described here. Illness anxiety disorder involves preoccupation without deliberate symptom production, and conversion disorder involves unexplained neurologic symptoms.
items.reference: Somatic Symptom Disorder: "In factitious illnesses, the patient consciously and voluntarily produces physical symptoms of illness... Common presentations of factitious disorder include chronic diarrhea, fever of unknown origin, GI bleeding or hematuria, seizures, and hypoglycemia."

items.subtopic: OCD — Caudate Nucleus and Treatment Response
items.question: Functional imaging studies of patients with obsessive-compulsive disorder who respond to successful treatment (pharmacologic or behavioral) typically show which change?
options: Increased metabolic activity in the caudate nucleus
Decreased metabolic activity in the caudate nucleus
No change in any brain region
Increased activity exclusively in the cerebellum
items.explanation: Interventions that successfully reduce obsessive-compulsive behaviors — whether pharmacologic or behavioral — are associated with decreased metabolic activity in the caudate nucleus, consistent with the caudate's role in the orbitofrontal-caudate-globus pallidus circuit implicated in OCD.
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder): "interventions that are successful in reducing obsessive-compulsive behaviors also decrease metabolic activity in the caudate."

items.question: Which combination of findings has been identified as predictive biomarkers in individuals at risk for developing PTSD after trauma exposure?
options: Decreased heart rate and serum lactate, with increased coagulation
Increased heart rate and serum lactate, decreased coagulation, and insulin resistance
Normal heart rate with isolated hypocalcemia
Decreased cortisol with increased coagulation only
items.explanation: Predictive biomarkers for PTSD include increased heart rate and serum lactate, decreased coagulation, insulin resistance, and alterations in glycolysis and fatty acid uptake — reflecting a broader metabolic signature of chronic stress dysregulation in PTSD.
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Stress Disorders): "Predictive biomarkers include increased heart rate and serum lactate, decreased coagulation, insulin resistance, and alterations in glycolysis and fatty acid uptake."

items.statement: Agoraphobia can only be diagnosed in patients who also meet criteria for panic disorder.
items.explanation: Agoraphobia, an acquired irrational fear of being in places where escape might be difficult, occurs commonly in patients with panic disorder but may be diagnosed even if panic disorder is not present.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Panic Disorder): "It may, however, be diagnosed even if panic disorder is not present."

items.statement: Family studies indicate that generalized anxiety disorder and panic disorder segregate together within families, suggesting a shared genetic basis.
items.explanation: Family studies indicate that GAD and panic disorder segregate independently, suggesting they are genetically distinct entities despite some symptom overlap.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "Interestingly, family studies indicate that GAD and panic disorder segregate independently."

items.statement: When discontinuing a long-acting benzodiazepine after chronic use, the dose should typically be reduced by about 10% every 1-2 weeks over 6-12 weeks.
items.explanation: Gradual stepwise dose reduction of approximately 10% every 1-2 weeks over a period of 6-12 weeks is the recommended approach for discontinuing longer-acting benzodiazepines to minimize withdrawal symptoms and relapse of anxiety.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "Withdrawal from the longer half-life benzodiazepines can be accomplished through gradual, stepwise dose reduction (by 10% every 1-2 weeks) over 6-12 weeks."

items.statement: Paroxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine CR, and venlafaxine XR are all FDA approved for the treatment of social anxiety disorder.
items.explanation: Several SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine CR) and the SNRI venlafaxine XR are FDA approved for social anxiety disorder, in addition to MAOIs which alleviate social phobia independent of their antidepressant activity.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Phobic Disorders): "paroxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine CR, and venlafaxine XR are FDA approved for social anxiety."

items.statement: Formal psychological debriefing immediately after a traumatic event has been shown to prevent the development of PTSD.
items.explanation: Debriefing after a traumatic event does not prevent PTSD and may, in some cases, worsen symptoms — a counterintuitive but well-established finding that argues against routine post-trauma debriefing as a preventive intervention.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Stress Disorders): "debriefing after the traumatic event does not prevent PTSD and may worsen symptoms."

items.statement: In PTSD, the pituitary-adrenal (HPA axis) response to trauma-related cues is exaggerated, paralleling the heightened sympathetic noradrenergic response.
items.explanation: Despite a heightened sympathetic/noradrenergic response to trauma cues in PTSD, the pituitary-adrenal (HPA axis/cortisol) response is paradoxically blunted — a dissociation between the two stress systems.
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Stress Disorders): "Greater sympathetic responses to cues associated with the traumatic event occur in PTSD, although pituitary adrenal responses are blunted."

items.statement: Patients with OCD commonly volunteer information about their obsessions and compulsions without being specifically asked.
items.explanation: Patients with OCD often conceal their symptoms out of embarrassment, so physicians must ask specific questions about recurrent thoughts and behaviors, especially when physical clues (e.g., chafed hands from excessive washing, patchy hair loss from trichotillomania) are present.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder): "Patients often conceal their symptoms, usually because they are embarrassed by the content of their thoughts or the nature of their actions. Physicians must ask specific questions regarding recurrent thoughts and behaviors, particularly if physical clues such as chafed and reddened hands or patchy hair loss... are present."

items.statement: Major depression is typically associated with increased REM latency and increased stage IV delta slow-wave sleep.
items.explanation: Major depression is associated with decreased (not increased) REM latency, increased REM density, and decreased (not increased) stage IV delta slow-wave sleep — characteristic sleep architecture abnormalities.
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Depressive Disorders): "decreased REM latency, increased REM density, and decreased stage IV delta slow-wave sleep."

items.statement: Depressed cardiac patients tend to show decreased heart rate variability, reflecting reduced parasympathetic activity, which may predispose to ventricular arrhythmia.
items.explanation: Depressed patients often show decreased heart rate variability, an index of reduced parasympathetic nervous system activity, which may predispose individuals to ventricular arrhythmia and increased morbidity.
items.reference: Depression in Association with Medical Illness: "Depressed patients often show decreased variability in heart rate (an index of reduced parasympathetic nervous system activity), which may predispose individuals to ventricular arrhythmia and increased morbidity."

items.statement: According to the STAR-D trial findings discussed, switching to an antidepressant within the same class is generally more effective than switching to a different class after non-response.
items.explanation: The STAR-D trial found that switching to an alternative antidepressant from the same class or a different class is equally likely to be effective, meaning there is no inherent advantage to staying within the same class.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Depressive Disorders): "In switching to a different monotherapy, other drugs from the same class appear to be as likely to be efficacious as choosing a drug from a different class. A large randomized trial (STAR-D) was unable to show preferential efficacy."

items.statement: Antidepressants are generally favored as first-line maintenance agents in bipolar disorder because they reduce the frequency of mood episodes.
items.explanation: Antidepressants are generally avoided during bipolar maintenance treatment because prolonged antidepressant use can iatrogenically trigger or accelerate rapid cycling and precipitate mania.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations (Bipolar Disorder): "rapid cycling... is iatrogenically triggered by prolonged antidepressant treatment."

items.statement: There is a specific pharmacologic agent that has been demonstrated to be broadly beneficial for the core symptoms of anorexia nervosa itself.
items.explanation: No pharmacologic intervention has been shown to be specifically beneficial for anorexia nervosa itself, although medications are used to treat comorbid conditions such as depression and anxiety. MANTRA and eating disorder-focused CBT are the primary effective treatments.
items.reference: Anorexia Nervosa (Treatment): "no pharmacologic intervention is specifically beneficial for anorexia itself."

items.statement: Unlike most other antipsychotics, clozapine does not cause an increase in prolactin levels.
items.explanation: Clozapine, unlike other antipsychotics with stronger D2 receptor blockade, does not cause a rise in prolactin levels — a distinguishing pharmacologic feature.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Schizophrenia): "Unlike other antipsychotics, clozapine does not cause a rise in prolactin levels."

items.assertion: Both noradrenergic and serotonergic antidepressants are effective in treating panic disorder.
items.reason: Provocative challenges that trigger panic attacks (sodium lactate, yohimbine, CCK-4, CO2) are hypothesized to activate a pathway involving noradrenergic neurons of the locus coeruleus and serotonergic neurons of the dorsal raphe.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion: because panic provocation involves both noradrenergic (locus coeruleus) and serotonergic (dorsal raphe) pathways, antidepressants acting on either system can be effective.
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Panic Disorder): "Each of these effects is hypothesized to occur via a pathway that involves noradrenergic neurons in the locus coeruleus and serotonergic neurons in the dorsal raphe, which may explain why antidepressants with noradrenergic and serotonergic activity are effective in the treatment of this disorder."

items.assertion: Buspirone is generally rated as less effective by patients who have previously responded well to a benzodiazepine.
items.reason: Buspirone has a high potential for abuse and significantly potentiates the sedative effects of alcohol.
items.explanation: The assertion is true — patients previously responsive to benzodiazepines tend to rate buspirone as less effective, in part due to its delayed onset. However, the reason is false: buspirone has no abuse potential and does not potentiate alcohol, which is one of its advantages over benzodiazepines.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): "Buspirone... has no abuse or disinhibition potential and does not potentiate the effects of alcohol... Patients who were previously responsive to a benzodiazepine are unlikely to rate buspirone as equally effective."

items.assertion: There is a genetic contribution to the development of phobic disorders.
items.reason: Twin studies of fear conditioning, an animal model for the acquisition of phobias, have demonstrated heritability of 35-45%.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion: the heritability demonstrated for fear conditioning (an experimental model relevant to phobia acquisition) supports the broader genetic contribution to phobic disorder vulnerability shown in twin concordance studies (23% MZ vs 15% DZ).
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Phobic Disorders): "A twin study of fear conditioning, an animal model for the acquisition of phobias, demonstrated heritability of 35-45%."

items.assertion: A diagnosis of PTSD can be made one week after a traumatic event in a patient with intrusive memories, avoidance, and hyperarousal.
items.reason: PTSD requires that the disturbance, regardless of duration, causes significant distress or impairment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — PTSD requires that the duration of the disturbance (Criteria B, C, D, and E) be more than 1 month (Criterion F); at one week, the presentation would more likely represent acute stress disorder. The reason itself is true: PTSD does require significant distress or impairment (Criterion G), but it alone does not make the one-week diagnosis correct.
items.reference: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Individuals Older Than 6 Years (Criterion F and G): "Duration of the disturbance (Criteria B, C, D, and E) is more than 1 month. ... The disturbance causes clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning."

items.assertion: Successful treatment of OCD, whether pharmacologic or behavioral, is associated with decreased metabolic activity in the caudate nucleus.
items.reason: The caudate nucleus is part of the cortico-striato-pallidal circuit (orbitofrontal cortex-caudate-globus pallidus) implicated in the anatomy of obsessive-compulsive behavior.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion: because the caudate is a key node in the circuit underlying OCD's repetitive behaviors, successful treatment that disrupts pathological obsessive-compulsive activity is reflected as decreased caudate metabolic activity.
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology (Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder): "The anatomy of obsessive-compulsive behavior is thought to include the orbital frontal cortex, caudate nucleus, and globus pallidus... interventions that are successful in reducing obsessive-compulsive behaviors also decrease metabolic activity in the caudate."

items.assertion: A grieving person who occasionally experiences positive emotions and humor while reminiscing about the deceased is unlikely to be experiencing a major depressive episode.
items.reason: The pain of grief may be accompanied by positive emotions and humor that are uncharacteristic of the pervasive unhappiness and misery characteristic of a major depressive episode.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion: the preservation of positive emotion and humor is one of the features that distinguishes normal grief from the pervasive misery of a major depressive episode, although clinical judgment is still needed since the two can coexist.
items.reference: Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (discussion): "The pain of grief may be accompanied by positive emotions and humor that are uncharacteristic of the pervasive unhappiness and misery characteristic of an MDE."

items.assertion: The high prevalence of depression in pancreatic cancer patients is fully explained by cachexia.
items.reason: Patients with pancreatic cancer have a higher prevalence of depression than patients with advanced gastric cancer, who also commonly experience cachexia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — the association between pancreatic cancer and depression is not due to cachexia alone. The reason is true: the higher prevalence of depression in pancreatic cancer persists even compared to gastric cancer patients with similar cachexia, which is precisely the evidence against the cachexia-only explanation.
items.reference: Depression in Association with Medical Illness: "This association is not due to the effect of cachexia alone, as the higher prevalence of depression in patients with pancreatic cancer persists when compared to those with advanced gastric cancer."

items.assertion: SSRIs are reasonable first-line antidepressants for patients with bundle branch block.
items.reason: SSRIs do not induce ECG changes or adverse cardiac events, unlike TCAs which are contraindicated in bundle branch block.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion: because SSRIs lack the cardiac conduction effects of TCAs (which are contraindicated in bundle branch block), they are a reasonable first-line choice in such patients — with appropriate monitoring for anticoagulant interactions if relevant.
items.reference: Depression in Association with Medical Illness: "TCAs are contraindicated in patients with bundle branch block... SSRIs appear not to induce ECG changes or adverse cardiac events and thus are reasonable first-line drugs for patients at risk for TCA-related complications."

items.assertion: Lithium is considered to have a unique protective effect against suicide among mood stabilizers.
items.reason: A sustained blood lithium level of at least 1.5 meq/L is required to achieve this antisuicidal effect.
items.explanation: The assertion is true — lithium has a demonstrated suicide-risk-reducing effect not yet shown for other mood stabilizers. The reason is false: the level required for prophylaxis and suicide risk reduction is at least 0.8 meq/L, not 1.5 meq/L (which would approach toxic levels, as the therapeutic range is 0.8-1.2 meq/L).
items.reference: TREATMENT (Bipolar Disorder): "A sustained blood lithium level of at least 0.8 meq/L is important for optimal prophylaxis and has been shown to reduce the risk of suicide."

items.assertion: Patients started on TCAs for major depression with active suicidal ideation should be given only a 10-day supply at a time.
items.reason: TCA overdose is generally non-lethal and poses minimal cardiac risk compared to SSRIs.
items.explanation: The assertion is true — limiting TCA prescriptions to a 10-day supply is judicious when suicide risk is present. The reason is false: TCA overdose can be lethal (desipramine carries the greatest risk), which is precisely why prescription quantities are limited, not because overdose is benign.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Depressive Disorders): "overdoses of tricyclic agents can be lethal, with desipramine carrying the greatest risk. It is judicious to prescribe only a 10-day supply when suicide is a risk."

items.assertion: Untreated anorexia nervosa carries the highest mortality rate among psychiatric disorders.
items.reason: Anorexia nervosa produces multisystem medical complications including metabolic alkalosis, hypokalemia, bradycardia, and hypercortisolemia, alongside elevated suicide risk.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion: the combination of severe multisystem metabolic/cardiovascular/endocrine derangement and elevated suicide risk accounts for anorexia's mortality of 5.1/1000, the highest of any psychiatric condition.
items.reference: Anorexia Nervosa: "Untreated anorexia has a mortality of 5.1/1000, the highest among psychiatric conditions."

items.assertion: Clozapine is generally reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia rather than used as a first-line agent.
items.reason: Clozapine carries risks of agranulocytosis and seizures that require intensive monitoring, including weekly white blood cell counts during the first 3 months of treatment.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion: the demanding monitoring requirements and serious adverse effect profile (agranulocytosis 1%, seizures 10%) of clozapine are why it is reserved for treatment-resistant cases despite its superior efficacy (~30% response in resistant patients) and suicide-prevention benefit.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Schizophrenia): "Approximately 30% of patients who do not respond to conventional antipsychotics will respond to clozapine... The most serious adverse effects of clozapine are agranulocytosis... and induction of seizures... Weekly white blood cell counts are required, particularly during the first 3 months of treatment."

items.title: Prevalence and Treatment Gap in Psychiatric Disorders
items.content: Psychiatric disorders are prevalent, with ~30% of the U.S. population affected by mental or substance use disorders. However, only one-third of those affected receive treatment.
keyPoints: Prevalence of psychiatric disorders in the U.S. is approximately 30%
Only one-third of individuals with these disorders are receiving treatment
Psychiatric conditions contribute to 4 out of the top 10 causes of morbidity and healthcare costs worldwide
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Psychiatric Disorders

items.title: Criteria for Referral to Psychiatry in Primary Care
items.content: Primary care physicians should refer patients to psychiatrists based on specific signs and symptoms rather than the absence of physical explanations. Indications include psychotic symptoms, mania, severe depression or anxiety, PTSD, suicidal or homicidal thoughts, or lack of response to initial treatment.
keyPoints: Referral is appropriate for psychotic symptoms, mania, severe depression, severe anxiety, PTSD, suicidal/homicidal preoccupation
Indication also includes failure to respond to first-order treatments
Physicians should communicate the reasons for referral and ensure continued medical care collaboration
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Psychiatric Disorders

items.title: Understanding the Global Impact of Mental Disorders
items.content: Mental and substance abuse disorders are significant contributors to disability worldwide, affecting over 300 million individuals. Despite differences in diagnostic systems like DSM-5-TR and ICD-10-CM, these conditions remain a leading cause of life-years lost to disability.
keyPoints: Mental and substance abuse disorders affect >300 million individuals globally.
These disorders are the major cause of life-years lost to disability among all medical illnesses.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Challenges in Mental Health Care Delivery
items.content: High-income countries need to enhance professional training in low- and middle-income countries to provide balanced care models for mental disorders. The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the exacerbation of unmet mental health needs due to societal disruption.
keyPoints: Capacity building is needed in professional training for mental health care in low- and middle-income countries.
COVID-19 increased incidence of psychiatric disorders, highlighting exacerbated unmet mental health needs.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Prevalence and Initial Evaluation of Anxiety Disorders
items.content: Anxiety disorders are highly prevalent, affecting 15–20% of patients in medical clinics. The clinician must first determine if anxiety is primary or secondary to a medical condition or medication side effect.
keyPoints: Anxiety disorders affect 15-20% of medical clinic patients.
Clinicians should evaluate whether anxiety antedates, postdates, or results from a medical illness or medication.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · ANXIETY DISORDERS

items.title: Medical Etiology in Anxiety Disorders
items.content: In approximately one-third of patients presenting with anxiety, there is a medical cause for the psychiatric symptoms. However, an anxiety disorder can also present with somatic symptoms without a diagnosable medical condition.
keyPoints: About one-third of anxious patients have a medical etiology for their psychiatric symptoms.
Anxiety disorders may present with somatic symptoms even in the absence of a diagnosable medical condition.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · ANXIETY DISORDERS

items.title: Characteristics of Panic Disorder Attacks
items.content: Panic disorder is characterized by recurrent, unpredictable panic attacks that include intense fear and physical symptoms such as palpitations, sweating, trembling, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, and a fear of impending doom. These episodes develop suddenly within 10 minutes and typically resolve in an hour.
keyPoints: Panic disorder has a lifetime prevalence of 2–3%.
Attacks have sudden onset, developing within 10 min.
Typically resolve over the course of an hour.
First attack usually occurs outside the home in late adolescence to early adulthood.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · PANIC DISORDER

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Comorbidity
items.content: Diagnosis requires at least 1 month of concern or behavioral change due to attacks. Panic disorder often coexists with other psychiatric conditions, including major depression in about 75% of patients.
keyPoints: At least 1 month of concern or behavior change needed for diagnosis.
Comorbidity: 75% will also meet criteria for major depression at some point.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · PANIC DISORDER

items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Etiology
items.content: Diagnosis involves ruling out medical etiologies. Panic disorder may be misdiagnosed due to comorbid conditions or self-medication attempts with substances like alcohol and benzodiazepines.
keyPoints: Rule out cardiovascular, respiratory, endocrine, and neurologic causes.
20% of syncope patients have a primary diagnosis of mood/anxiety/substance abuse disorder, often panic disorder.
Etiology involves genetic predisposition, altered autonomic responsivity, and social learning.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · PANIC DISORDER

items.title: SSRIs as First-Line Treatment for Panic Disorder
items.content: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are the cornerstone of pharmacotherapy in panic disorder, offering efficacy without the adverse effects associated with tricyclic antidepressants. They should be initiated at lower doses than typically used for depression.
keyPoints: SSRIs like fluoxetine, paroxetine, sertraline, and venlafaxine are FDA-approved for panic disorder.
Start SSRIs at one-third to one-half of usual antidepressant dose (e.g., 5–10 mg fluoxetine, 25–50 mg sertraline).
Antidepressants take 2-6 weeks to become effective; doses may need adjustment based on response.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Panic Disorder

items.title: Role of Benzodiazepines and Psychotherapy in Panic Disorder Management
items.content: Benzodiazepines provide immediate relief for panic symptoms, useful early in treatment. Psychological therapies enhance drug treatment effectiveness through education and exposure techniques.
keyPoints: FDA-approved benzodiazepines include alprazolam and clonazepam; effective for anticipatory anxiety and symptom relief.
Cochrane review found no difference between antidepressants and benzodiazepines in response rate.
Psychotherapy involves 12-15 sessions, with techniques like breathing exercises and exposure to precipitating events.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Panic Disorder

items.subtopic: GENERALIZED ANXIETY DISORDER | Generalized Anxiety Disorder
items.title: Understanding the Prevalence and Comorbidity of GAD
items.content: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) is characterized by persistent, excessive worry. It typically begins before age 20 years, with a lifetime prevalence of 5–6%. There's a higher risk among first-degree relatives. More than 80% of patients with GAD also have major depression, dysthymia, or social phobia.
keyPoints: Onset usually before age 20 years
Lifetime prevalence: 5-6%
More than 80% comorbid with major depression, dysthymia, or social phobia
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · GENERALIZED ANXIETY DISORDER | Generalized Anxiety Disorder

items.subtopic: GENERALIZED ANXIETY DISORDER | Generalized Anxiety Disorder
items.title: Pharmacologic Treatment of GAD: Benzodiazepines
items.content: Benzodiazepines are effective for short-term management of GAD, with lorazepam, oxazepam, clonazepam, and alprazolam being preferred. They should be used at the lowest dose possible on an as-needed basis, not exceeding 4–6 weeks to avoid tolerance and dependence.
keyPoints: Preferred benzodiazepines: lorazepam, oxazepam, clonazepam, alprazolam
Initiate treatment at the lowest dose possible
Prescribe for no more than 4-6 weeks
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · GENERALIZED ANXIETY DISORDER | Generalized Anxiety Disorder

items.subtopic: GENERALIZED ANXIETY DISORDER | Generalized Anxiety Disorder
items.title: Managing Benzodiazepine Withdrawal in GAD Patients
items.content: Withdrawal from benzodiazepines, especially those with longer half-lives like diazepam and clonazepam, should be managed through gradual dose reduction. Shorter-acting agents may require switching to a longer half-life benzodiazepine or adjunctive medications before tapering.
keyPoints: Gradual dose reduction by 10% every 1–2 weeks
Switch to longer half-life benzodiazepines if needed
Use adjunctive medication like beta blockers or carbamazepine
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · GENERALIZED ANXIETY DISORDER | Generalized Anxiety Disorder

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-5 Diagnostic Criteria for Generalized Anxiety Disorder
items.title: Duration and Symptomatology of GAD
items.content: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) is characterized by excessive anxiety and worry occurring more days than not for at least six months. The diagnosis requires the presence of three or more specific symptoms, with some present for over half that duration.
keyPoints: Excessive anxiety must occur more days than not for at least 6 months.
Worry is about a number of events or activities (e.g., work, school).
Three or more symptoms from the list: restlessness, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance.
In children, only one symptom is required.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-5 Diagnostic Criteria for Generalized Anxiety Disorder

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-5 Diagnostic Criteria for Generalized Anxiety Disorder
items.title: Treatment Considerations and Medication Options for GAD
items.content: For treating GAD, several medications are FDA approved, including escitalopram, paroxetine, duloxetine, and venlafaxine. Buspirone is an alternative but less effective than benzodiazepines in some cases. Benzodiazepines should be avoided during pregnancy and breastfeeding.
keyPoints: Escitalopram, paroxetine, duloxetine, and venlafaxine are FDA approved for GAD treatment.
Buspirone has no abuse potential but requires several weeks to take effect.
Benzodiazepines contraindicated during pregnancy and breastfeeding.
Anticonvulsants like gabapentin and pregabalin may be used off-label.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-5 Diagnostic Criteria for Generalized Anxiety Disorder

items.title: Understanding the Prevalence and Demographics of Phobic Disorders
items.content: Phobic disorders are common, affecting approximately 7–9% of the population. There is a notable gender disparity, with twice as many females affected as males. These disorders often first meet full diagnostic criteria in early adulthood, although avoidance behaviors can begin in childhood.
keyPoints: Prevalence: Affects ~7–9% of the population.
Gender Disparity: Twice as many females are affected than males.
Onset: Full diagnostic criteria usually met in early adulthood; avoidance behaviors may start in childhood.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · PHOBIC DISORDERS | Phobic Disorders

items.title: Treatment Approaches for Phobic Disorders
items.content: Effective treatment of phobic disorders often involves a combination of medication and psychotherapy. Beta blockers are useful for performance anxiety by blocking peripheral symptoms, while MAOIs and certain SSRIs like paroxetine have FDA approval for social phobia. Cognitive-behavioral strategies focus on altering distorted perceptions to reduce fear.
keyPoints: Beta Blockers: Effective for performance anxiety (e.g., propranolol 20–40 mg orally 2 h before the event).
Medications: MAOIs and SSRIs like paroxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine CR, venlafaxine XR approved for social phobia.
Psychotherapy: Cognitive-behavioral strategies are crucial; focus on altering perceptions of fear-producing stimuli.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · PHOBIC DISORDERS | Phobic Disorders

items.subtopic: STRESS DISORDERS | Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Individuals Older Than 6 Years
items.title: Understanding PTSD Prevalence and Risk Factors
items.content: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) affects a significant portion of the population, with prevalence rates between 5% and 10%. Women are more likely to develop PTSD than men. Key risk factors include past psychiatric history, high neuroticism, extroversion, and genetic predisposition.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 5-10% of Americans will experience PTSD at some point in their lives.
Gender Disparity: Women are more likely to be affected than men.
Risk Factors: Past psychiatric history, high neuroticism, extroversion, and genetic predisposition.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · STRESS DISORDERS | Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Individuals Older Than 6 Years

items.subtopic: STRESS DISORDERS | Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Individuals Older Than 6 Years
items.title: Recognizing PTSD Symptoms and Associated Disorders
items.content: PTSD symptoms include anxiety, detachment, loss of emotional responsibility, depersonalization, and reexperiencing trauma through intrusions, dreams, or flashbacks. Patients often avoid trauma-related stimuli and exhibit increased vigilance, arousal, and startle response. They are also at risk for developing other disorders such as anxiety, mood disorders, and substance abuse.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Anxiety, detachment, loss of emotional responsibility, depersonalization, reexperiencing trauma.
Avoidance: Active avoidance of trauma-related stimuli.
Increased Vigilance: Heightened arousal and startle response.
Associated Disorders: Increased risk for anxiety, mood disorders, and substance abuse.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · STRESS DISORDERS | Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Individuals Older Than 6 Years

items.subtopic: For children 6 years and younger, see corresponding criteria below.
items.title: Understanding Trauma Exposure in Young Children
items.content: Trauma exposure for children under six can occur through direct experience, witnessing events involving close ones, or repeated exposure to traumatic details. Criterion A4 excludes media exposure unless work-related.
keyPoints: Direct experiencing of trauma
Witnessing trauma involving family/friends
Repeated exposure to aversive details (e.g., first responders)
Criterion A4 does not apply to media exposure, except if work-related
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · For children 6 years and younger, see corresponding criteria below.

items.subtopic: For children 6 years and younger, see corresponding criteria below.
items.title: Intrusion Symptoms in Young Children Post-Trauma
items.content: Children may exhibit recurrent distressing memories or dreams related to trauma. In those older than six, repetitive play may express traumatic themes. Frightening dreams without recognizable content are also possible.
keyPoints: Recurrent, involuntary intrusive memories
Recurrent distressing dreams
Dissociative reactions (e.g., flashbacks)
Trauma-specific reenactment in play for children
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · For children 6 years and younger, see corresponding criteria below.

items.subtopic: For children 6 years and younger, see corresponding criteria below.
items.title: Duration and Impact of Traumatic Disturbances
items.content: The disturbance must last more than one month and cause significant distress or impairment. It should not be due to substances or other medical conditions.
keyPoints: Disturbance duration: more than 1 month
Clinically significant distress or impairment
Not attributable to substance effects or another medical condition
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · For children 6 years and younger, see corresponding criteria below.

items.subtopic: Specify whether: | Specify if: | Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Children 6 Years and Younger
items.title: Recognizing Dissociative Symptoms in PTSD for Young Children
items.content: In children aged 6 years and younger, dissociative symptoms such as depersonalization or derealization can be indicative of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). These symptoms must not be attributable to substance use or other medical conditions.
keyPoints: Depersonalization: Feeling detached from one's mental processes or body.
Derealization: Experiencing surroundings as unreal, dreamlike, distant, or distorted.
Dissociative symptoms should not result from substances or other medical conditions.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Specify whether: | Specify if: | Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Children 6 Years and Younger

items.subtopic: Specify whether: | Specify if: | Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Children 6 Years and Younger
items.title: Understanding Intrusion Symptoms in PTSD for Young Children
items.content: In children aged 6 years and younger, intrusion symptoms such as recurrent distressing memories or trauma-specific reenactments are key indicators of PTSD. These may manifest through play rather than verbal expression.
keyPoints: Recurrent, involuntary, intrusive distressing memories of the traumatic event(s).
Trauma-specific reenactment in play.
Spontaneous and intrusive memories may not appear distressing.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Specify whether: | Specify if: | Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Children 6 Years and Younger

items.subtopic: Persistent Avoidance of Stimuli | Stress Disorders
items.title: Mechanisms Underlying Persistent Avoidance in PTSD
items.content: In PTSD, avoidance behaviors are linked to excessive norepinephrine release from the locus coeruleus and increased noradrenergic activity at hippocampal and amygdala projection sites. These changes facilitate fear-based memory encoding.
keyPoints: Excessive release of norepinephrine from the locus coeruleus
Increased noradrenergic activity in the hippocampus and amygdala
Facilitation of fear-based memory encoding
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Persistent Avoidance of Stimuli | Stress Disorders

items.subtopic: Persistent Avoidance of Stimuli | Stress Disorders
items.title: Treatment Approaches for PTSD and Avoidance Behaviors
items.content: PTSD treatment involves SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline), venlafaxine, fluoxetine, topiramate, and psychotherapeutic strategies like trauma-focused CBT, EMDR, and prolonged exposure therapy. MDMA and stellate ganglion block are adjuncts to intensive psychotherapy.
keyPoints: SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline) FDA approved for PTSD
Venlafaxine, fluoxetine, topiramate reduce anxiety and avoidance behaviors
Psychotherapeutic strategies: trauma-focused CBT, EMDR, prolonged exposure therapy
Adjuncts: MDMA, stellate ganglion block
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Persistent Avoidance of Stimuli | Stress Disorders

items.title: Diagnosis and Recognition of OCD
items.content: OCD is characterized by obsessive thoughts and compulsive behaviors that significantly impair daily functioning. It often involves fears such as contamination, leading to excessive handwashing or checking behaviors. These activities typically consume more than 1 hour per day. Patients may hide their symptoms due to embarrassment.
keyPoints: Obsessive-compulsive activities take up >1 h per day
Common comorbid conditions include depression and other anxiety disorders
Lifetime prevalence of OCD is 2–3% worldwide
Onset usually in early adulthood, but childhood onset is not rare
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · OBSESSIVE-COMPULSIVE DISORDER | Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder

items.title: Treatment Approaches for OCD
items.content: Pharmacotherapy with SSRIs like fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, paroxetine, and sertraline is effective in treating OCD. Clomipramine, a TCA, is also used but has more side effects. Only 50–60% of patients respond adequately to pharmacotherapy alone. In resistant cases, augmentation strategies or interventions like rTMS may be beneficial.
keyPoints: Clomipramine dosage: 25–250 mg/d
Fluoxetine dosage: 5–60 mg/d
Fluvoxamine dosage: 25–300 mg/d
Paroxetine dosage: 40–60 mg/d
Sertraline dosage: 50–150 mg/d
Only 50–60% show adequate improvement with pharmacotherapy alone
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · OBSESSIVE-COMPULSIVE DISORDER | Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder

items.title: Major Depressive Disorder vs. Bipolar Disorder: Key Differentiators and Impact on Global Health
items.content: Mood disorders encompass depressive, bipolar, and depression associated with medical conditions or substance abuse. Major depressive disorder (MDD) is distinguished from bipolar disorder by the absence of manic or hypomanic episodes. The familial relationship between MDD and bipolar disorder suggests a higher prevalence of MDD in families with bipolar individuals, but not vice versa. Depression significantly contributes to global disability, as evidenced by its status as the leading cause of disability in the 2019 Global Burden of Disease Study.
keyPoints: MDD is differentiated from bipolar disorder by the absence of manic or hypomanic episodes.
Depression was the largest factor contributing to disability in the 2019 WHO study.
Disability-adjusted life-years (DALYs) for depression increased by 61% since 1990.
Lost productivity due to mood disorders in the U.S. is estimated at $55.1 billion per year.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · MOOD DISORDERS

items.subtopic: ■ DEPRESSION IN ASSOCIATION WITH MEDICAL ILLNESS
items.title: Depression in Cardiac Patients: Impact on Outcomes and Treatment Considerations
items.content: Depression is prevalent among cardiac patients, with 20-30% manifesting a depressive disorder. Depressive symptoms can impair rehabilitation and are linked to higher mortality and morbidity rates. Depression may reduce heart rate variability, increasing the risk of ventricular arrhythmia.
keyPoints: 20-30% of cardiac patients have a depressive disorder.
Depression impairs rehabilitation and increases mortality/morbidity.
Decreased heart rate variability in depressed patients predisposes to ventricular arrhythmia.
TCAs are contraindicated in bundle branch block; SSRIs preferred for cardiac risk.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · ■ DEPRESSION IN ASSOCIATION WITH MEDICAL ILLNESS

items.subtopic: ■ DEPRESSION IN ASSOCIATION WITH MEDICAL ILLNESS
items.title: Depression and Cancer: Prevalence, Impact, and Treatment
items.content: Depression is common in cancer patients, with a mean prevalence of 25%. It occurs more frequently (40-50%) in pancreatic or oropharyngeal cancers. Antidepressant medication can improve quality of life and mood, while psychotherapy may help short-term symptoms.
keyPoints: Mean depression prevalence in cancer patients is 25%.
Depression occurs in 40-50% of pancreatic/oropharyngeal cancer patients.
Antidepressants improve quality of life; psychotherapy aids short-term symptoms.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · ■ DEPRESSION IN ASSOCIATION WITH MEDICAL ILLNESS

items.subtopic: ■ DEPRESSION IN ASSOCIATION WITH MEDICAL ILLNESS
items.title: Depression in Diabetes: Prevalence, Correlation with Hyperglycemia, and Treatment Challenges
items.content: Depression prevalence in diabetes ranges from 8-27%, correlating with hyperglycemia severity. Antidepressant treatment can affect glycemic control; MAOIs may induce hypoglycemia, while TCAs can cause hyperglycemia.
keyPoints: Depression prevalence in diabetics: 8-27%.
Severity of mood correlates with hyperglycemia and complications.
MAOIs can cause hypoglycemia; TCAs may lead to hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · ■ DEPRESSION IN ASSOCIATION WITH MEDICAL ILLNESS

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Major Depression
items.content: Major depression is diagnosed when a patient exhibits a depressed mood daily for at least two weeks, accompanied by symptoms such as changes in sleep and appetite, fatigue, impaired concentration, feelings of guilt or worthlessness, and thoughts of death. The presence of anhedonia (loss of pleasure) and early-morning awakening are key differentiators from normal sadness.
keyPoints: Depressed mood for a minimum duration of 2 weeks
Associated symptoms: changes in sleep patterns, appetite, weight; motor agitation or retardation; fatigue; impaired concentration; feelings of shame or guilt; thoughts of death
Anhedonia and early-morning awakening are characteristic
Diurnal variation in mood (worse in morning hours)
15% lifetime prevalence of major depressive episodes
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · DEPRESSIVE DISORDERS

items.title: Differentiating Depression from Bereavement
items.content: While bereavement can mimic depression with overlapping symptoms, it is typically characterized by feelings of emptiness and loss rather than anhedonia or diminished self-esteem. The duration of grief-related symptoms is usually shorter compared to major depression.
keyPoints: Bereavement emphasizes feelings of emptiness and loss
Major depression involves anhedonia and loss of self-esteem
Duration of bereavement symptoms is generally limited
In some cases, major depression may be diagnosed even with significant loss
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · DEPRESSIVE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 1)
items.title: Criteria for Diagnosing a Major Depressive Episode
items.content: To diagnose a major depressive episode, five or more specific symptoms must be present during the same two-week period and represent a change from previous functioning. At least one of these symptoms must be either depressed mood or loss of interest/pleasure.
keyPoints: Five (or more) symptoms required within a 2-week period
At least one symptom: (1) depressed mood or (2) loss of interest/pleasure

Symptoms include: depressed mood, diminished interest, weight change, sleep disturbances, psychomotor changes, fatigue, feelings of worthlessness, concentration issues, and recurrent thoughts of death
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 1)
items.title: Differentiating Grief from Major Depressive Episode
items.content: Grief and major depressive episodes can present with similar symptoms, but key differences include the nature of affect, intensity over time, presence of positive emotions, self-esteem preservation in grief, and focus of thoughts.
keyPoints: Grief: feelings of emptiness/loss; waves of dysphoria tied to reminders
MDE: persistent depressed mood; inability to anticipate happiness
Grief may include humor and preserved self-esteem; MDE involves pervasive unhappiness, worthlessness
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 1)
items.title: Exclusionary Criteria for Major Depressive Episode
items.content: A major depressive episode must not be better explained by schizoaffective disorder or superimposed on other psychotic disorders, and there should never have been a manic or hypomanic episode unless substance-induced.
keyPoints: Not attributable to schizoaffective disorder or schizophrenia spectrum
No history of manic/hypomanic episodes unless substance-induced
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 2)
items.title: Identifying High-Risk Patients for Suicide
items.content: When suspecting major depression, assess suicide risk through direct questioning. Identify specific plans or significant risk factors such as past attempts, hopelessness, medical illness, substance abuse, or social isolation.
keyPoints: Approximately 4–5% of depressed patients commit suicide.
Most suicidal patients seek help within one month before their deaths.
Risk factors include past suicide attempts, profound hopelessness, concurrent medical illness, substance abuse, and social isolation.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Persistent Depressive Disorder
items.content: Persistent depressive disorder involves chronic symptoms lasting at least two years. Symptoms are often less severe than major depression but can have significant functional consequences.
keyPoints: Occurs in ~2% of the general population.
Symptoms may be less severe/numerous compared to major depression, yet functionally impactful.
Can coexist with major depressive episodes ('double depression').
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 2)
items.title: Etiology and Pathophysiology of Unipolar Depression
items.content: Unipolar depression involves genetic, neuroendocrine, and environmental factors. Genetic studies show higher concordance in monozygotic twins compared to dizygotic siblings.
keyPoints: Monozygotic twin concordance rate: 46%.
Dizygotic sibling concordance rate: 20%.
Neuroendocrine abnormalities include increased cortisol and CRH, decreased glucocorticoid response to dexamethasone, and blunted TSH response to TRH.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 3) | Depressive Disorders (part 1)
items.title: Understanding the Biologic Rhythms in Major Depression
items.content: Major depression is associated with specific changes in sleep patterns, such as decreased REM latency and increased REM density. These alterations suggest that disruptions in biological rhythms may be a core aspect of the disorder.
keyPoints: Decrease in rapid eye movement (REM)-sleep onset (REM latency)
Increase in REM density
Possible decrease in stage IV delta slow-wave sleep
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 3) | Depressive Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 3) | Depressive Disorders (part 1)
items.title: Antidepressant Mechanisms and Treatment Planning
items.content: While antidepressants inhibit neurotransmitter uptake quickly, their therapeutic effects develop over weeks due to adaptive changes in cellular mechanisms. Effective treatment involves both medication and psychotherapy, with a focus on early intervention and support.
keyPoints: Therapeutic effects of antidepressants emerge over several weeks
Approximately 40% of primary care patients drop out if no improvement is seen within a month
Between 60 and 70% respond to any drug given in sufficient dose for 6–8 weeks
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 3) | Depressive Disorders (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 3) | Depressive Disorders (part 1)
items.title: Selecting Antidepressants: Considerations and Safety
items.content: Choosing an antidepressant involves considering patient preference, medical history, and potential side effects. TCAs require careful monitoring due to toxicity risks, while second-generation drugs offer different profiles with specific considerations.
keyPoints: TCAs have antihistaminergic and anticholinergic side effects
TCAs contraindicated in patients with serious cardiovascular risk factors
SSRIs cause fewer anticholinergic and sedating side effects but may lead to serotonin syndrome
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · TABLE 463-7 Criteria for a Major Depressive Episode (part 3) | Depressive Disorders (part 1)

items.title: SSRI Selection and Monitoring
items.content: When choosing an SSRI for treating depressive disorders, consider factors such as half-life, compliance, toxicity, and drug-drug interactions. Fluoxetine has a long half-life of almost 7 days, while paroxetine may cause more withdrawal symptoms if discontinued abruptly.
keyPoints: Fluoxetine's combined half-life with norfluoxetine is almost 7 days.
Steady-state levels for fluoxetine are achieved in about 5 weeks.
Paroxetine has a higher risk of withdrawal symptoms upon abrupt discontinuation.
All SSRIs may impair sexual function, potentially leading to noncompliance.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Depressive Disorders (part 2)

items.title: Managing SSRI-Induced Sexual Dysfunction
items.content: Sexual dysfunction is a common side effect of SSRIs and can lead to noncompliance. Strategies such as dose reduction, weekend drug holidays, or adjunct medications like amantadine may help mitigate these effects.
keyPoints: SSRIs commonly cause sexual dysfunction, including diminished libido and impotence.
Consider lowering the SSRI dose or instituting weekend drug holidays to manage side effects.
Amantadine (100 mg/day) can be used to ameliorate sexual dysfunction.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Depressive Disorders (part 2)

items.title: SSRI Drug-Drug Interactions and Cytochrome P450 Inhibition
items.content: Most SSRIs, except citalopram and escitalopram, inhibit cytochrome P450 enzymes, affecting the metabolism of other medications. Fluoxetine and paroxetine inhibit 2D6, impacting drugs like type 1C antiarrhythmics.
keyPoints: Fluoxetine and paroxetine inhibit CYP2D6, affecting drug levels of certain medications.
Sertraline affects CYP3A4, potentially altering blood levels of carbamazepine or digoxin.
Concomitant use with MAOIs is contraindicated due to the risk of serotonin syndrome.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Depressive Disorders (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Options for Treatment-Resistant Depression
items.content: Various interventions are available for treatment-resistant depression, including electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), vagus nerve stimulation (VNS), intravenous ketamine and intranasal esketamine, psilocybin, and deep brain stimulation. These treatments have shown efficacy in controlled trials or meta-analyses.
keyPoints: ECT is effective for treatment-resistant cases and delusional depressions.
rTMS is approved for treatment-resistant depression with proven efficacy.
VNS has controversial efficacy but is approved for treatment-resistant depression.
Intravenous ketamine and intranasal esketamine show short-term antidepressant effects, often after a single administration.
Psilocybin shows potential benefit in controlled settings.
Deep brain stimulation targets specific brain regions with demonstrable efficacy.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Depressive Disorders (part 3)

items.title: Management of Postpartum Depression
items.content: Postpartum depression can be managed using standard treatments for depression, but brexanolone offers a unique approach. Administered as a continuous intravenous infusion over 60 hours, it provides symptomatic relief for at least 30 days, with an oral version available for outpatient use.
keyPoints: Brexanolone administered over 60 hours can relieve postpartum depression symptoms for at least 30 days.
Sedation and loss of consciousness are possible adverse effects of brexanolone.
An oral form, zuranolone, is available for outpatient treatment.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Depressive Disorders (part 3)

items.title: Evaluating Treatment Response and Strategies for Resistance
items.content: Treatment response should be evaluated after approximately two months. If remission is inadequate, consider patient compliance and medication dose adjustments. For treatment resistance, alternative drugs, combinations of antidepressants, or adjunctive treatments may be used.
keyPoints: Evaluate treatment response after ~2 months; three-quarters of patients show improvement by this time.
If remission is inadequate, assess compliance and consider increasing medication doses if side effects are manageable.

Strategies for resistance include alternative drugs, antidepressant combinations, or adjunctive treatments with lithium, thyroid hormone, L-methylfolate, S-adenosylmethionine, N-acetyl cysteine, atypical antipsychotics, and dopamine agonists.
Addition of certain atypical antipsychotic drugs (quetiapine extended-release; aripiprazole; brexpiprazole) has FDA approval for treatment-resistant depression.
Olanzapine and fluoxetine combination (Symbyax) is approved for use.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Depressive Disorders (part 3)

items.title: Recognizing Bipolar Disorder: Key Features and Diagnostic Challenges
items.content: Bipolar disorder is characterized by mood swings between mania/hypomania and depression. Recognizing bipolarity in patients presenting with depressive episodes without a history of mania is crucial, as antidepressants may worsen symptoms or accelerate cycling.
keyPoints: Onset typically occurs between 20-30 years but can be earlier.
~1.5% prevalence in the U.S. population.
Women more likely to have depressive episodes; men more manic episodes over a lifetime.
Suggestive features of bipolarity include childhood onset, treatment failure with antidepressants, hypersomnolence, weight gain, irritability, or impulsivity.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · BIPOLAR DISORDER

items.title: Understanding Rapid Cycling in Bipolar Disorder
items.content: Rapid cycling is defined as having four or more episodes of depression or mania within a year. It occurs in 15% of patients, predominantly women, and can be linked to thyroid dysfunction or triggered by prolonged antidepressant treatment.
keyPoints: Rapid cycling affects ~15% of bipolar disorder patients.
More common in women.
Linked to thyroid dysfunction or iatrogenic triggers like antidepressants.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · BIPOLAR DISORDER

items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Etiology of Bipolar Disorder
items.content: The differential diagnosis for mania includes secondary causes such as drug use, hyperthyroidism, and neurological disorders. Genetic predisposition is significant, with a high concordance rate in monozygotic twins.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes stimulant drugs, hyperthyroidism, neurologic disorders like Huntington's or Wilson's disease.
Genetic predisposition: ~80% concordance in monozygotic twins.
Risk genes overlap with schizophrenia and autism.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · BIPOLAR DISORDER

items.title: Lithium: Mainstay of Bipolar Treatment with Prophylactic Benefits
items.content: Lithium carbonate is the primary treatment for bipolar disorder, effective in 70-80% of acute mania cases. It also helps prevent recurrent manic and depressive episodes, though it's underutilized.
keyPoints: Response rate to lithium in acute mania: 70–80%
Onset of beneficial effects: 1–2 weeks
Prophylactic effect for recurrent mania and depression
Therapeutic blood level range: 0.8–1.2 meq/L
Reduces risk of suicide with sustained levels ≥0.8 meq/L
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Bipolar Disorder

items.title: Initiation and Monitoring of Lithium Therapy in Acute Mania
items.content: Lithium is initiated at 300 mg bid or tid, with dose adjustments every 2-3 days to achieve therapeutic levels. Close monitoring is essential due to the narrow margin between therapeutic and toxic levels.
keyPoints: Initial lithium dose: 300 mg bid or tid
Dose increase: 300 mg every 2–3 days
Target blood level: 0.8–1.2 meq/L
Adjunctive agents for agitation: lorazepam (1–2 mg q4h) or clonazepam (0.5–1 mg q4h)
Monitoring necessity due to close therapeutic-toxicity levels
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Bipolar Disorder

items.title: Valproic Acid for Rapid Cycling and Mixed Mania in Bipolar Disorder
items.content: Valproic acid is preferred over lithium for patients with rapid cycling or mixed mania, offering effective management of these challenging presentations.
keyPoints: More effective than lithium for rapid cycling (≥4 episodes/year) or mixed/dysphoric mania
Common side effects: tremor and weight gain
Rare toxicities: hepatotoxicity and pancreatitis
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Bipolar Disorder

items.title: Duration and Severity Thresholds for Manic Episodes
items.content: A manic episode requires a distinct period of elevated or irritable mood with increased activity lasting at least one week, or any duration if hospitalization is necessary. This period must include three (or more) specific symptoms to a significant degree.
keyPoints: Duration: At least 1 week (or any duration if hospitalization is required).

Symptoms: Three (or four if irritable mood) of the following - inflated self-esteem, decreased need for sleep, increased talkativeness, flight of ideas, distractibility, increased goal-directed activity or psychomotor agitation, excessive involvement in high-risk activities.
Severity: Must cause marked impairment in functioning or necessitate hospitalization, or include psychotic features.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Manic Episode

items.title: Criteria for Bipolar I Disorder Diagnosis
items.content: A diagnosis of bipolar I disorder requires at least one lifetime manic episode. A full manic episode emerging during antidepressant treatment but persisting beyond its physiological effects is sufficient evidence for a manic episode.
keyPoints: Lifetime occurrence: At least one manic episode required for bipolar I diagnosis.
Antidepressant-induced mania: If a manic episode emerges during and persists after antidepressant treatment, it qualifies as a manic episode.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Manic Episode

items.title: Duration and Symptom Criteria for Hypomanic Episodes
items.content: A hypomanic episode is characterized by a distinct period of elevated, expansive, or irritable mood along with increased activity or energy lasting at least 4 consecutive days. During this time, three (or more) specific symptoms must be present to a significant degree and represent a noticeable change from usual behavior.
keyPoints: Duration: At least 4 consecutive days

Symptoms: Three (or more if mood is irritable) of the following - inflated self-esteem or grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, increased talkativeness, flight of ideas, distractibility, increase in goal-directed activity or psychomotor agitation, excessive involvement in high-risk activities.
Change from usual behavior
Observable by others
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Hypomanic Episode

items.title: Distinguishing Features and Diagnostic Considerations for Hypomania
items.content: A hypomanic episode is not severe enough to cause marked impairment in social or occupational functioning or necessitate hospitalization. It should not be attributable to substances or other medical conditions. Caution is advised when diagnosing based on symptoms emerging during antidepressant treatment.
keyPoints: Not severe enough for marked impairment or hospitalization
No psychotic features (otherwise it's manic)
Not due to substance effects or another medical condition
Caution with antidepressant-induced symptoms
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Hypomanic Episode

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Major Depressive Episode
items.content: A major depressive episode is diagnosed when five or more specific symptoms are present during the same 2-week period, representing a change from previous functioning. At least one of these must be either depressed mood or loss of interest/pleasure.
keyPoints: Five (or more) symptoms required over a 2-week period
At least one symptom must be: (1) depressed mood or (2) loss of interest/pleasure
Symptoms should not be attributable to another medical condition
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Major Depressive Episode

items.title: Clinical Impact and Exclusion Criteria for Major Depressive Episodes
items.content: The symptoms must cause significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning. They should not be due to the physiological effects of a substance or another medical condition.
keyPoints: Symptoms cause clinically significant distress or impairment
Not attributable to substances or other medical conditions
Consideration needed for symptoms following significant loss
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Major Depressive Episode

items.subtopic: Bipolar I Disorder | SOMATIC SYMPTOM DISORDER
items.title: Diagnosis of Bipolar I Disorder: Importance of Excluding Other Psychotic Disorders
items.content: To diagnose Bipolar I Disorder, it is crucial to confirm that a manic episode has occurred and ensure it cannot be better explained by other psychotic disorders such as schizoaffective disorder or schizophrenia. This distinction helps guide appropriate treatment strategies.
keyPoints: At least one manic episode must meet criteria A–D under 'Manic Episode'.

The manic episode should not be better explained by schizoaffective disorder, schizophrenia, schizophreniaiform disorder, delusional disorder, or other specified/unspecified schizophrenia spectrum and psychotic disorders.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Bipolar I Disorder | SOMATIC SYMPTOM DISORDER

items.subtopic: Bipolar I Disorder | SOMATIC SYMPTOM DISORDER
items.title: Characteristics of Somatic Symptom Disorder: Prevalence and Diagnostic Considerations
items.content: Somatic Symptom Disorder is prevalent in 5–7% of patients presenting with distressing somatic symptoms. The diagnosis focuses on the patient's interpretation rather than the absence of medical explanation, recognizing that comorbid conditions like depression or anxiety are common.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 5–7% of patients in general practice experience significant preoccupation with somatic symptoms.
Diagnosis emphasizes patient's interpretation and elaboration of symptoms over lack of medical explanation.
Comorbidity with depressive and anxiety disorders is frequent, impacting severity and functional outcomes.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Bipolar I Disorder | SOMATIC SYMPTOM DISORDER

items.subtopic: Somatic Symptom Disorder and Related Disorders | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PICA
items.title: Approach to Somatic Symptom Disorder: Behavior Modification Over Diagnostic Testing
items.content: In managing somatic symptom disorder, the focus should be on behavior modification rather than extensive diagnostic testing or surgeries. This involves regulating physician access and providing consistent support without linking it directly to patient distress levels.
keyPoints: Diagnostic tests and exploratory surgeries are ineffective for somatic symptom disorder.
Behavior modification is key, with regulated physician access.
Visits should be brief and not contingent on diagnostic or treatment actions.
Antidepressant treatment may benefit some patients.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Somatic Symptom Disorder and Related Disorders | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PICA

items.subtopic: Somatic Symptom Disorder and Related Disorders | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PICA
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Pica
items.content: Pica is diagnosed in individuals aged >2 years who consume nonnutritive, nonfood substances for over a month, requiring medical attention. It must be differentiated from culturally approved practices and is not typically linked to nutritional deficiencies.
keyPoints: Diagnosis of pica requires ingestion of nonnutritive substances for more than one month in individuals >2 years old.
Medical attention is necessary due to the behavior.
Exclude culturally approved practices when diagnosing.
Common onset is in childhood, but can occur with psychiatric conditions in adults.
Association with pregnancy noted; diagnosis requires increased medical risks.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Somatic Symptom Disorder and Related Disorders | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PICA

items.subtopic: RUMINATION DISORDER | AVOIDANT/RESTRICTIVE FOOD INTAKE DISORDER | ANOREXIA NERVOSA
items.title: Characteristics and Onset of Ruminative Behavior in Infants
items.content: Rumination disorder involves the repeated regurgitation, rechewing, or spitting out of food without an underlying medical condition. This behavior typically occurs daily for at least one month and can lead to weight loss and malnutrition.
keyPoints: Behavior must persist for at least 1 month.
Onset in infancy is usually between 3 and 12 months of age.
Weight loss and malnutrition are common sequelae.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · RUMINATION DISORDER | AVOIDANT/RESTRICTIVE FOOD INTAKE DISORDER | ANOREXIA NERVOSA

items.subtopic: RUMINATION DISORDER | AVOIDANT/RESTRICTIVE FOOD INTAKE DISORDER | ANOREXIA NERVOSA
items.title: Differentiating Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder from Anorexia Nervosa
items.content: Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder is characterized by avoidance or restriction of food intake due to lack of interest or distaste, without the emotional factors like fear of weight gain seen in anorexia nervosa.
keyPoints: Emotional factors such as fear of gaining weight and body image distortion are present in anorexia nervosa but not in avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder.
Onset is usually in infancy or early childhood for both disorders.
Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder can be comorbid with anxiety, cognitive, and attention-deficit disorders.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · RUMINATION DISORDER | AVOIDANT/RESTRICTIVE FOOD INTAKE DISORDER | ANOREXIA NERVOSA

items.subtopic: RUMINATION DISORDER | AVOIDANT/RESTRICTIVE FOOD INTAKE DISORDER | ANOREXIA NERVOSA
items.title: Clinical Features and Management of Anorexia Nervosa
items.content: Anorexia nervosa involves significant weight loss due to caloric restriction, fear of gaining weight, and body image disturbance. It is associated with multisystemic medical consequences and requires careful management to avoid refeeding syndrome.
keyPoints: Body weight significantly deviates from norms based on age, gender, health, and developmental standards.
Lifetime prevalence in women can be up to 4%.
Mortality rate of untreated anorexia nervosa is 5.1/1000, the highest among psychiatric conditions.
Weight gain should be gradual at 0.5–1 pound per week to prevent refeeding syndrome.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · RUMINATION DISORDER | AVOIDANT/RESTRICTIVE FOOD INTAKE DISORDER | ANOREXIA NERVOSA

items.title: Characteristics and Prevalence of Bulimia Nervosa
items.content: Bulimia nervosa involves recurrent binge eating (at least once a week for 3 months) followed by compensatory behaviors to avoid weight gain. It affects individuals who are typically normal weight or slightly overweight, with disturbances in body image and episodes often triggered by negative self-esteem or social stress.
keyPoints: Binge eating occurs at least once a week for 3 months.
Compensatory behaviors include purging, use of laxatives, excessive exercise.
Lifetime prevalence in women is ~2%, with a 10:1 female-to-male ratio.
Medical risks include fluid and electrolyte disturbances and cardiac conduction abnormalities.
Effective treatments involve SSRI antidepressants combined with psychotherapy.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · BULIMIA NERVOSA | BINGE-EATING DISORDER

items.title: Distinguishing Features of Binge-Eating Disorder
items.content: Binge-eating disorder is characterized by episodes of binge eating without subsequent compensatory behaviors, unlike bulimia nervosa. It involves distress and loss of control over eating, with a lower female-to-male ratio compared to bulimia.
keyPoints: Absence of compensatory behaviors after binge eating.
12-month prevalence in females is 1.6%.
Lower female-to-male ratio than bulimia nervosa.
Prognosis is better than other eating disorders, with rare transitions to other conditions.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · BULIMIA NERVOSA | BINGE-EATING DISORDER

items.title: Personality Disorders: Differentiating from Medical Conditions
items.content: Personality disorders are characterized by inflexible patterns of thinking, feeling, and behavior that cause significant impairment or distress. It is crucial to differentiate these from changes due to medical conditions like neurologic or endocrine illnesses, as personality change can be an early sign of such diseases.
keyPoints: Personality disorders must not be secondary to another mental disorder, substance abuse, or a general medical condition.
Frontal-lobe tumors may present with motivation and personality changes while neurologic examination remains normal.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Personality Disorders: Clusters and Comorbidity Risks
items.content: Personality disorders are categorized into three clusters based on shared characteristics. Cluster A includes odd/eccentric behaviors, Cluster B involves impulsive/emotional behavior, and Cluster C is characterized by anxiety/fear. There's an increased risk of comorbid major mental disorders in patients with personality disorders.
keyPoints: Cluster A: paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal; odd/eccentric behaviors.
Cluster B: antisocial, borderline, histrionic, narcissistic; impulsive/emotional behavior.
Cluster C: avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive; anxiety/fear traits.
Increased risk of comorbid major mental disorder in patients with personality disorders.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Genetic Contribution to Personality Disorders
items.content: Personality disorders have a genetic component, with studies identifying loci linked to neuroticism.
keyPoints: Genetic studies suggest a contribution to personality disorder development.
One study identified nine loci significantly linked to aspects of neuroticism.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | Personality Disorders | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Treatment Approaches for Personality Disorders
items.content: Dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) is effective in treating personality disorders, while certain medications can be used based on the cluster type.
keyPoints: DBT has demonstrated efficacy in randomized trials for personality disorders.
Antidepressants and low-dose antipsychotics are useful for cluster A disorders.
Anticonvulsant mood stabilizers and MAOIs may benefit cluster B patients with specific symptoms.
Cluster C patients often respond to medications used for axis I anxiety disorders.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | Personality Disorders | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Course of Schizophrenia
items.content: Schizophrenia is diagnosed based on the presence of specific symptoms over a defined period, with varying symptomatology affecting prognosis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis requires at least two positive or negative symptoms for 1 month and continuous signs for 6 months.
Negative symptoms are associated with poor long-term outcomes and drug response.
Schizophrenia prevalence is ~0.85% worldwide, with a lifetime prevalence of ~1–1.5%.
About 10% of patients commit suicide.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | Personality Disorders | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Identifying Drug-Induced Psychosis in New-Onset Cases
items.content: In cases of newly emergent psychosis, it is crucial to rule out drug causes as they can mimic psychotic symptoms. Common medications associated with such symptoms include parkinsonian drugs, clonidine, quinacrine, and procaine derivatives.
keyPoints: Drug reactions causing hallucinations or paranoia should be considered in new-onset psychosis.
Common culprits: Parkinsonian medications, clonidine, quinacrine, procaine derivatives.
Rule out drugs as a cause of newly emergent psychotic symptoms.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Genetic Risk Factors in Schizophrenia
items.content: Schizophrenia has a significant genetic component, with increased risk observed in first-degree relatives and monozygotic twins. The disorder is also linked to several hundred small effect risk loci identified through GWAS.
keyPoints: Risk for schizophrenia: ~6.6% in first-degree relatives of an affected proband.
If both parents are affected, offspring risk is 40%.
Concordance rates: 50% for monozygotic twins, 10% for dizygotic twins.
GWAS identifies small effect loci and larger copy number variants.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanism of Action and Target Receptors of Antipsychotics
items.content: Antipsychotic agents, both traditional and atypical, primarily function by blocking dopamine D2/D3 receptors in the ventral striatum. Atypical antipsychotics also target other receptors such as 5-HT1A, 5-HT7, mGlu2/3, muscarinic acetylcholine (M1, M4), and noradrenergic receptors to improve efficacy and reduce side effects.
keyPoints: Antipsychotics bind to dopamine D2/D3 receptors in the ventral striatum.
Clinical potency of traditional antipsychotics correlates with their affinity for D2 receptors.
Atypical agents target additional receptors like 5-HT1A, 5-HT7, mGlu2/3, and muscarinic acetylcholine (M1, M4).
Clozapine has a higher affinity for the D4 receptor than the D3 receptor.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Schizophrenia (part 1)

items.title: Efficacy and Side Effect Profiles of Antipsychotics
items.content: Conventional neuroleptics like chlorpromazine are more sedating, while higher-potency agents such as haloperidol may cause extrapyramidal side effects. Atypical antipsychotics like clozapine are preferred for treatment-resistant schizophrenia due to their efficacy in reducing suicide risk and treating negative symptoms, despite the risk of blood dyscrasias.
keyPoints: Older agents (e.g., chlorpromazine) cause sedation and orthostatic hypotension.
Higher-potency antipsychotics (e.g., haloperidol) are more likely to induce extrapyramidal side effects.
Clozapine is effective in 30% of treatment-resistant cases and reduces suicide risk but carries a risk of blood dyscrasias.
Atypical agents are more effective for negative symptoms and cognitive function.
items.reference: Psychiatric Disorders · Schizophrenia (part 1)

items.title: Dosage, Treatment Duration, and Relapse Prevention
